title: Media To Consume This Week Instead of Doomscrolling - By & For you
subtitle: Featuring a selection of some of the most interesting voices from this space: philosophy, short stories, zines, activism, essays, art, and so much more!!!
author: mapuc
raw_article:
The current systems, platforms, and algorithms that we navigate to get our work out, on and offline, are deeply flawed; there is SO much incredible work that simply goes unnoticed because of this very reason. I consider myself so lucky to have a platform where my voice is heard by so many (we’re almost at 500k <3), so I always want to make sure that I amplify others’ voices as well—especially the ones from the very people that have chosen to be part of this space.

Personally, rather than spending my energy on the current issues with the people that Substack is platforming now (yes, I am referring to the Andrew Tate situation), I want to turn this frustration into something productive, leveraging these systems for good, changing them from within.

Furthermore, the algorithmic homogenization of what makes it to our feeds isn’t only culturally flattening, but it keeps us disconnected from each other, from meaningful interactions with media and the people behind it. This is one of the main reasons this publication exists in the first place, and why I asked you to submit your work.

I have realized that community isn’t the same thing as an audience. This initiative is part of a shift toward actually building community, which means making sure there is not only dialogue between you and me, but between the people in this space. That’s why I want to create more dynamics like this one, and share more about myself and what goes on behind the scenes to make sure you have a say in the direction this publication goes.

To do this, I scheduled a live for Sunday the 26th—Interlude #1: on changing, purpose, and connection—hopefully the first of many.

This will be a far more personal and casual corner of this publication, where on this occassion, we get to have this conversation in depth, and where I fill you in on what this shift means. Hope to see you there!!!!

Add the live to your calendar :)

P.S. if you are reading this on email, it might be cut off. You can head to the web version to make sure you see the full thing!

Before you dive in, make sure to like, comment, and subscribe to the work from this selection that resonates with you. Consider doing the same for this publication if you feel called to it <3

I am back in my fandom era after many years of stepping away from it, but, genuinely, it has been the most grounding space as I navigate all the crazy things happening in my life and with my health. I have been reading fics pretty much every day for a couple of months, and I have come to cherish these moments of relief where I can just escape. People really undermine the value of these communities and women’s roles within them; it’s generally seen as cringey or shameful, but this was such a meaningful analysis of why it is perceived this way—I had never really seen anyone talk about it in such thorough detail, which I genuinely appreciate. #fangirl4ever

I want to take a moment to thank each and every person who participated, I tried to narrow the pieces down as best as I could, but there are so many gems I had to leave out—I got almost around 400 submissions so that’s why this took a while.

I hope you enjoyed this initiative as much as I did :) Feeling really proud of everyone involved, including myself <3

title: Finding Meaning Online Instead of Doomscrolling
subtitle: On experiencing digital and multimedia art to create positive feedback loops through connection.
author: mapuc
raw_article:
This post was created in collaboration with CIFRA. All opinions, curation, and creative direction are entirely my own. As always, I only recommend things that I genuinely think you will love :)

How many hours of your day do you spend in front of a screen? And what exactly are you doing during these hours?

I spend around six hours on my phone, give or take, and then between my iPad and my laptop, probably a few more. A couple of years ago, I would have been ashamed to tell you my answer—I mean, there’s a reason I created this space in the first place—but somewhere along the way, I realized my screen time wasn’t the issue. The fact that meaning collapsed was.

There was a lack of meaning in my reality. The future laid out before me, and the one I watched unfold through every screen was not one I wanted to experience. I couldn’t spend enough time with my own thoughts to figure out who I was or what I enjoyed anymore; my love for art, music, philosophy, writing, films, poetry, and everything in between had gone out the window. Nothing really mattered anymore.

For a long time, I attempted to escape existence by sticking this digital pacifier in front of my face, losing track of space and time for long enough to forget about reality for a few hours. Every single day, for years. It felt like living a severed life, one where two halves of a self existed in my body, one physical and one digital; both unable to access the other’s memories. All I knew was that both felt a profound sense of impending doom—in other words, I was doomscrolling.

Doomscrolling is merely one symptom of much deeper systemic issues. As Baudrillard writes in Simulacra and Simulation:

“We live in a world where there is more and more information, and less and less meaning.”

Especially at this moment in history, when the boundaries between the digital and the physical are being increasingly dissolved, consumption, perception, and attention define the conception of our reality—and most of the systems we navigate daily are designed to exploit exactly that. For me, this meant I had to integrate those two versions of myself, jump right into the state of seemingly meaningless entropy, and somehow build a reality worth living for myself. Because who is in charge of designating meaning anyway? I decided it wasn’t going to be an algorithm.

Meaning, to me, is the result of connection, which, frustratingly, has never come easily—my neurodivergent brain wasn’t built to do so in the way some others can. But, as I mentioned previously, I always felt connected through media and art. When the human is at the center, they become the shared language that allows me to connect not only with others—including you—but to all versions of myself.

Once I decided to navigate the digital world rather than letting it navigate me, these all converged in ways I could have never imagined—visual art bleeding into music into film into literature into performance, all finding each other through the digital medium in forms I couldn’t have conceived of separately. Video art, sound installation, interactive media, net art—the boundaries between my rediscovered passions dissolved the same way the boundaries between the physical and digital are. This is how I discovered the universe of contemporary and multimedia digital art.

As wonderful as I found it to be, pieces were scattered all over niche corners of the internet, many of which are overwhelming to navigate due to both volume and inaccessibility—and unfortunately, authorship often got lost in the process. Given that much of it is abstract, conceptual, and experimental work that takes us out of our comfort zones, it’s even more difficult to know where to start.

Thankfully, though, I eventually stumbled upon CIFRA—a human-curated platform where we actually get to experience and integrate this world of digital and contemporary art in our daily lives. This once amorphous distant world is made navigable by centering intentional curation, authorship, and context, to allow us to meaningfully engage with art rather than stay stuck in passive consumption during our time spent online.

We stand at a critical moment in history where the humanities are being defunded, and creativity feels increasingly scarce. The STEM fields are separating themselves from art, dismissing its value in the name of efficiency and so-called progress—as if technology were a replacement for what we can create rather than a tool to amplify it. That’s why supporting artists and the spaces that are actively working against this is more important than ever—and what makes CIFRA particularly meaningful to me. Through CIFRA CLUB, their optional membership, 51% of our subscriptions go directly to the artists.

Finding meaning is at the center of everything I’ve described—but what does this actually entail?

Here’s the framework that allowed me to make sense of it:

  • Passive consumption dictated by the attention economy, where agency, critical thought, and self-determination are diminished, creates negative feedback loops, where scrolling, disconnection, and meaninglessness remain.

  • Active consumption led by human connection, creation, and curation allows positive feedback loops to emerge, where personal interrelatedness with the objects of our attention constructs meaning through experience.

At the core of my conception of meaning in this context, I have to ask myself how diversified multimedia interactions can create positive feedback loops. We watch, read, and listen to so many things every day, but these are all futile unless we create connections.

I connect with a piece of media when I am able to fit it into the ever-growing jigsaw that constructs my reality. It connects with me once it fits into the other pieces of the puzzle that have held significance in my life, all filtered through personal experience and perception.

To illustrate this, I curated a playlist on CIFRA where we go on a multimedia art journey of feedback loops between the pieces featured in this playlist and my curated selection of books—an encounter of the digital with the analog as an ode to sense-making through the interconnectedness perceived through my body:

Feedback Loops

  1. Cosmic Body - Prelude for Rational Freshness - Orbital By Samantha Harvey

  2. Political Body - Object - Gender Trouble by Judith Butler

  3. Systemic Body - Veille infinie - 24/7 by Jonathan Crary

  4. Autonomous Body - re-c(O)unting - Breasts and Eggs by Mieko Kawakami

  5. Fragmented Body - Deep Ocean Blue - Bluets by Maggie Nelson

  6. Existential Body - Everything Is Absolutely Fine - Nausea by Jean-Paul Sartre

  7. Contextual Body - This Happened Yet Is Happening - Mythologies by Roland Barthes

  8. Decaying Body - subassemblies - Annihilation by Jeff VanderMeer

  9. Essential Body - Singularity - When We Cease to Understand the World by Benjamín Labatut

A little piece of myself to inspire you to create meaning when everything around us feels meaningless <3

I wholeheartedly believe that art will always save us—I know it has saved me more than once. If this resonated with you, you can find the playlist here or download CIFRA for free and find Feedback Loops there. And if you want to directly support the artists behind these works, consider joining CIFRA CLUB—51% of every subscription goes straight to them :)

How many hours of your day do you spend in front of a screen? And what exactly are you doing during these hours?

I spend around six hours on my phone, give or take, and then between my iPad and my laptop, probably a few more. A couple of years ago, I would have been ashamed to tell you my answer—I mean, there’s a reason I created this space in the first place—but somewhere along the way, I realizedmy screen time wasn’t the issue.The fact that meaning collapsed was.

There was a lack of meaning in my reality. The future laid out before me, and the one I watched unfold through every screen was not one I wanted to experience. I couldn’t spend enough time with my own thoughts to figure out who I was or what I enjoyed anymore; my love for art, music, philosophy, writing, films, poetry, and everything in between had gone out the window. Nothing really mattered anymore.

For a long time, I attempted to escape existence by sticking this digital pacifier in front of my face, losing track of space and time for long enough to forget about reality for a few hours. Every single day, for years. It felt like living a severed life, one where two halves of a self existed in my body, one physical and one digital; both unable to access the other’s memories. All I knew was that both felt a profound sense of impending doom—in other words, I was doomscrolling.
Doomscrolling is merely one symptom of much deeper systemic issues. As Baudrillard writes in Simulacra and Simulation:
“We live in a world where there is more and more information, and less and less meaning.”

Especially at this moment in history, when the boundaries between the digital and the physical are being increasingly dissolved, consumption, perception, and attention define the conception of our reality—and most of the systems we navigate daily are designed to exploit exactly that.For me, this meant I had to integrate those two versions of myself, jump right into the state of seemingly meaningless entropy, and somehow build a reality worth living for myself. Becausewho is in charge of designating meaning anyway?I decided it wasn’t going to be an algorithm.

Meaning, to me, is the result of connection, which, frustratingly,has never come easily—my neurodivergent brain wasn’t built to do so in the way some others can. But, as I mentioned previously, I always felt connected through media and art. When the human is at the center, they become the shared language that allows me to connect not only with others—including you—but to all versions of myself.

Once I decided to navigate the digital world rather than letting it navigate me, these all converged in ways I could have never imagined—visual art bleeding into music into film into literature into performance, all finding each other through the digital medium in forms I couldn’t have conceived of separately. Video art, sound installation, interactive media, net art—the boundaries between my rediscovered passions dissolved the same way the boundaries between the physical and digital are. This is how I discovered the universe of contemporary and multimedia digital art.

As wonderful as I found it to be, pieces were scattered all over niche corners of the internet, many of which are overwhelming to navigate due to both volume and inaccessibility—and unfortunately, authorship often got lost in the process. Given that much of it is abstract, conceptual, and experimental work that takes us out of our comfort zones, it’s even more difficult to know where to start.

title: How to Drift Through the Indie Web Instead of Doomscrolling
subtitle: There are corners of the internet that still feel alive; you just have to know where to look. A digital dérive with Alex C. from Blog Therapy
author: mapuc
raw_article:
You might notice some changes around here, more on that coming soon ꩜ For now, I’ll leave you to enjoy the first ever collaboration on this publication!

I was very lonely growing up. As an only child with undiagnosed AuDHD, let’s just say I had to come to terms with the fear that I might not actually belong anywhere—or at least anywhere I could go at the time. However, once I was older, but probably not old enough to have unrestricted access to the internet, I finally found a place where I truly felt I could be myself. In between my Adventure Time fan account, my attempts at writing fan fiction on Wattpad, and a mildly successful Rainbow Loom Instagram account, I felt so alive—and so did the internet back then.

A few years down the line, the internet felt dead—just as I did. Its state of algorithmic soullessness had seemed to rub off on me and the more I scrolled, the farther I got from that place where I once felt at home. Looking back, though, I was the one who found and created spaces within the internet where I could be myself to the fullest extent of my expression, and not the other way around.

By the time I was old enough to leave my hometown, I had made the choice to move halfway across the globe. I left behind everything familiar because I refused to conform to a life that was not for me, to settle for a world that felt too small. Dare I say the World Wide Web is no different—if you want to discover it, you have to be willing to actually move through it.

In my quest toward figuring out why today’s internet makes us feel so numb—and most importantly, what to do about it—I began exploring the concept of dérive: a Situationist theory and practice developed in 1950s Paris. It was carried out by a group of avant-garde artists and political theorists, consisting of drifting through the spaces between the beaten paths without a destination. This is as a form of resistance against the spectacle-fueled systems designed to keep us on predetermined routes.

How could this logic apply online? I knew the indie web was the perfect place to bring the concept of digital derivé from theory to practice, but I simply didn’t know where to start. That’s when I reached out to Alexandra, a freelance editor and writer covering digital culture, tech, and alternative online spaces. She runsBlog Therapy here on Substack, where you can find essays on these topics, plus regular dispatches from the indie web and interviews with netizens.

Before we dive in, I’d like to share with you how Alex illustrated the essence of the indie web to me:

“The indie web is the orchard that blooms wild outside the gated estate of mainstream social media.”

Blog Therapy
Deep dives into digital culture, languages, and tech. Book reviews. Curiosity holes. A newsletter in progress.
By BlogTherapy

Alex has worked on indie web-related pieces for New_Public, like this article and this guided tour. She also works with small websites, like Untested, and freelances as a newsletter editor. In her spare time, she’s a voracious reader and urbex enthusiast.

My latest installment of Things To Do Instead of Doomscrolling is live over on Perfectly Imperfect. Titled Giving/Receiving, and very on theme with today's post, I share ways reciprocity has shaped my life lately, on and offline <3

Things To Do Instead of Doomscrolling

Alex's Guide to the Indie Web

Thanks, Mapu, for reaching out with this collaboration. I love the idea of digital dérive on the indie web because, in my view, they are a perfect match. The alt web was built for purposeful exploration at one’s own pace.

There are many reasons to get curious about what’s out there. Maybe you’re tired of doomscrolling, emotionally overwhelmed by the endless stream of content without context, without a break to digest what you’re seeing. Maybe you want to reclaim your attention span or discover new communities. Here are some great places to get started.

First off, if building and hosting your own site from scratch feels daunting, you can try indie blogging. Some great platforms for this are Pika, Bear Blog, WordPress, Ghost, and, of course, Tumblr (which is now federated). There are also Plume and Write.as, which are both part of the fediverse, meaning you can share your posts across other federated platforms like Mastodon.

All of these sites are free and private, though some, like WordPress, Ghost, and Pika, have paid tiers.

If you are interested in building your own site, there are a few accessible indie options: Neocities, WordPress (again), and Blot.im, which turns a Dropbox folder into a website by dragging and dropping. There is also Are.na, an indie alternative to Pinterest, and HotGlue.me, a visual webpage builder.

Either way, this is a good explainer of what a website is, how to build it, and what tools you can use. And the 32Bit Cafe has a comprehensive resource list broken down by skill level and how involved you want to get.

You can probably vibecode a site using something like Claude, but I wouldn’t recommend it. Instead, it would be very indie web, very punk, to try and pick up a new skill and make something with it from scratch.

Another important part of indie web infrastructure is the webring—a collection of sites organised in circular fashion, usually around a specific topic. This is one of the easiest ways to surf the alt web in search of new haunts. Some good ones to start with: this directory from IndieWeb.org (a fantastic resource in its own right), this list of webrings grouped by active/inactive sites, MelonKing’s Surf Club, and this Neocities webring.

There are also site directories, like this explorer from Marginalia.nu and this giant list from Bisray. Many personal sites also have a friends & recommendations sidebar, like the one on Kicks Condor’s site (on the righthand side as you scroll).

Some cool sites to inspire you include the aesthetically lush Satyrs’ Forest, the whimsical Olliveen, and this point-and-click SciFi novella.

But not everything on the indie web is a high-octane, visually ambitious art project. There are also personal sites and blogs where people talk about their day, gush about their new puppy, and post earnestly about their passions.

In the spirit of the indie web I would love for you to be a part of this community where I hope to change the world one attention span at a time :) If you want to dive even deeper and directly support all of the work and devotion I pour into this space, consider becoming a paid subscriber or buy me a book <3

Buy me a book ꩜

What Does Digital Dérive Actually Look Like?

After speaking to Alex and getting all of these wonderful resources, saying I was excited is an understatement. In fact, I don’t think words are enough to explain what this journey was about.

Before I sat down to spend my evening drifting through the indie web, I had this idea that I would walk you through the places I found in a linear way—showing the start and the destination, describing what each place was about. But the second I actually began jumping from one link to another, that hyper-structured vision fell apart.

At the end, I had countless saved links and no idea how to share them in a way that made sense. After giving it far too much thought, I realized the answer was right in front of me the whole time—I could make my own website and simply share them there. Easier said than done, but after the many hours of trial and error that any new skill requires, I created my own little corner of it on Neocities.

I approached this as an art piece, curating a selection of places that genuinely sparked something within me. Against all of my instincts to always provide precise and detailed descriptions, I gave myself the task of expressing what that spark looked like—restricting myself to just a few words per line. Without further preamble, here it is:

This took so long and I’m very proud come check it out

This was one of the most challenging yet rewarding creative projects I’ve done in a very long time. I particularly love how minimal it is—it directly opposes what I’m used to doing when I share media with you. Beyond visuals and long, detailed reflections, it’s about finding creative ways to spark curiosity without them. This is definitely a work in progress, but I’m excited to keep adding to it :)

I’d like to conclude this section with the haiku I wrote to accompany the dictionary of haikus—because in essence, it captures this whole experience far more efficiently than I’m trying to here.

wandering the web

surfing through all the pixels

at last i feel real

Your turn to write one ꩜

Leave a comment

Shared Reflections

On the perception of digital spaces: indie web v.s. mainstream media

We tend to have very black-and-white thinking when it comes to seemingly opposing concepts. It was challenging for me to visualize how the indie web and mainstream media platforms could coexist in the same space. When I asked Alex about her thoughts on this, she said that the indie web makes the online world feel bigger and more expansive. This doesn’t mean that it’s a replacement for mainstream social media, but it’s definitely an important part of a balanced media diet. I concluded that digital spaces are built mostly through our perception of them, so if we see them as separate, rather than a web of connections, there is no way to move between them and find genuine balance. I do believe it’s important to remember what makes these two different:

“The indie web is a space for curiosity, creativity, and autonomy, where you don’t have to put up with unsavoury tradeoffs just to participate (unlike on Instagram, TikTok, etc., where you have to hand over your personal data, your attention span, and even the rights to what you post). And there is a welcoming and active community, which has been growing over the past few years.”

The community aspect is the absolute highlight of the indie web to me. Alex spent last summer writing an intro to the indie web for New_Public and interviewed a bunch of people who host their own sites. She told me about how much she enjoyed their answers and insights, which just seems to echo my sentiment—one of her favorite responses was from the creator behind MelonKing, who called this period of rediscovery the “web revival”, a nod to the American Folk Revival. You can find some of these interviews up in full on Blog Therapy, and make sure to check out the latest one :)

Blog Therapy
Dispatch from the Indie Web #1
More interviews! The indie web is all about intentionally building community and there is no better to do that than just talking to people.Thanks for being here and subscribe for more indie web reporting…
Read more
a month ago · 2 likes · 1 comment · BlogTherapy

On the indie web and dérive as resistance

Ever since I started to actively try to improve my so-called “social media addiction”, I’ve just naturally made this move from subconscious to conscious awareness of the larger issue at hand, and in turn, have organically found myself in some corners of the indie web by drifting from link to link. I was and am still active on mainstream social media because I couldn’t look past the fact that, despite its many flaws, it could be used for good, and ultimately, that was how it was possible for me to build this space.

That’s why it actually felt refreshing when Alex pushed back on the concept of social media addiction. When I brought it up, she mentioned she finds calling it an addiction perpetuates misconceptions about the gap it’s attempting to fill—especially for young people. She then posed the following questions:

  • What if it’s actually an imperfect solution to a much bigger problem?

  • What if we’re chronically on social media not because of addiction, but because that’s where everything and everybody is?

She points out how these platforms monopolize and control our lives to the point that the real world is online now—for most, this means just a few apps run by the same three mega corporations.

However, she also brought up the point I mentioned earlier on, reframing our view of social media to foster community and resistance by highlighting all the fundraisers, protests, garage sales, buy-nothing groups, and marches organized via Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, etc.

To actually drive significant change by using social media as a powerful tool of connection and resistance, though, we must understand at a large scale that the loss of agency and attention are just the tip of the iceberg—mere symptoms of a much more complex underlying issue. Alex illustrates this very clearly: “Consider that when an authoritarian regime wants to hide what it’s doing to their own people, they cut off internet access. Because social media can be an impartial tool for truth-telling and democracy.”

Rather than dwelling on the impending doom that we’ve gotten used to, what if we stop waiting for someone else to do something about it and start with what we can actually control? That’s why I wanted to explore dérive in the first place, but without an infrastructure, that wouldn’t be possible. The indie web and the web revival at large IS the infrastructure—it’s “an online world that’s way more diverse, much broader, more privacy-friendly, more welcoming to creativity, self-expression, and autonomous exploration.”

On accessibility and getting started

When I asked Alex about the shifts she believed need to happen to actually make the indie web more accessible, and a genuine alternative to doomscrolling, here’s what she answered:

  1. Practical: “Many non-technical people are intimidated by the idea of building and hosting your own site. We need to build awareness about the existing options (some of which I mentioned earlier on) and work to create newer, better ones. Starting a site or blog shouldn’t be any more complicated than getting Gmail set up.”

  2. Psychological: “We need to move away from passive consumption to (pro)active discovery and outreach. Many of us just aren’t used to that anymore. On mainstream platforms, you don’t need to go looking for your interests, your people; the algorithm narrows down the content you get until it’s just right. This is convenient, but it also dulls curiosity and takes away our intellectual autonomy, our ability to discern about the content we consume”

  3. Personal: “It’s reclaiming our curiosity, our interests, and our desires. This requires a level of autonomy that’s incompatible with mindless doomscrolling. If you want to make the most of the indie web, you need to get back in touch with your interests, forget about being cringe, and follow your curiosity (online and off). There’s a powerful resistance here and I think a lot of us would benefit from it.”

Even though I was already familiar with some places on the indie web, a few of which I’ve shared with you previously, I had only scratched the surface. I wanted to show you how I practice what I preach by fully immersing myself in these spaces through the frameworks that resonate with me: curiosity-led research and consumption turning into creation. Regardless of all the challenges I faced during this project and the shifts that still need to happen—both personally and at large—it was more than worth it and I’m glad that I picked up a new hobby in the process.

I truly want the internet to feel alive and expressive rather than oppressive for more than just a few. My goal is always to make these shifts more accessible and achievable for as many people as possible, so I hope this piece tips the scales, even if just a little, toward a more creative, inclusive, curious, and indie future.

Some closing words from Alex on how the indie web has impacted her life:

I’m excited about being online again. Working in media for almost a decade now, I got pretty cynical about what’s out there. Sure, there was my work as an editor, and that was mostly fun (especially on the digital culture news desk), but the internet seemed to be shrinking.

But getting into the indie web, the online world seemed to grow again. I think a good comparison is hopping off the guided tour to get lost down random side streets and alleys instead. Wandering based on curiosity and intuition rather than being led by the hand to my next destination.

It’s really put me back in touch with my interests. Now, when I’m on Instagram for too long, I start to feel a bit dead inside and log off to play with my dog or something.

A big thank you to Alex for agreeing to be a part of this very special project, everyone please go send her some love!

Sending love to all of you who made it all the way here, I’m excited to share more upcoming collaborations and I hope you have lots of fun on your own digital dérive :)

Writing to you from my bed with my emotional support fidget egg,

Buy me a book ꩜

Leave a comment

Share

Before I sat down to spend my evening drifting through the indie web, I had this idea that I would walk you through the places I found in a linear way—showing the start and the destination, describing what each place was about. But the second I actually began jumping from one link to another, that hyper-structured vision fell apart.

At the end, I had countless saved links and no idea how to share them in a way that made sense. After giving it far too much thought, I realized the answer was  right in front of me the whole time—I could make my own website and simply share them there. Easier said than done, but after the many hours of trial and error that any new skill requires, I created my own little corner of it on Neocities.

I approached this as an art piece, curating a selection of places that genuinely sparked something within me. Against all of my instincts to always provide precise and detailed descriptions, I gave myself the task of expressing what that spark looked like—restricting myself to just a few words per line. Without further preamble, here it is:
This took so long and I’m very proud come check it out

This was one of the most challenging yet rewarding creative projects I’ve done in a very long time. I particularly love how minimal it is—it directly opposes what I’m used to doing when I share media with you. Beyond visuals and long, detailed reflections, it’s about finding creative ways to spark curiosity without them. This is definitely a work in progress, but I’m excited to keep adding to it :)
I’d like to conclude this section with the haiku I wrote to accompany the dictionary of haikus—because in essence, it captures this whole experience far more efficiently than I’m trying to here.
wandering the web
surfing through all the pixels
at last i feel real
Your turn to write one ꩜
Shared Reflections
On the perception of digital spaces: indie web v.s. mainstream media

We tend to have very black-and-white thinking when it comes to seemingly opposing concepts. It was challenging for me to visualize how the indie web and mainstream media platforms could coexist in the same space. When I asked Alex about her thoughts on this, she said that the indie web makes the online world feel bigger and more expansive. This doesn’t mean that it’s a replacement for mainstream social media, but it’s definitely an important part of a balanced media diet. I concluded that digital spaces are built mostly through our perception of them, so if we see them as separate, rather than a web of connections, there is no way to move between them and find genuine balance. I do believe it’s important to remember what makes these two different:

“The indie web is a space for curiosity, creativity, and autonomy, where you don’t have to put up with unsavoury tradeoffs just to participate (unlike on Instagram, TikTok, etc., where you have to hand over your personal data, your attention span, and even the rights to what you post). And there is a welcoming and active community, which has been growing over the past few years.”

The community aspect is  the absolute highlight of the indie web to me. Alex spent last summer writing an intro to the indie web for New_Public and interviewed a bunch of people who host their own sites. She told me about how much she enjoyed their answers and insights, which just seems to echo my sentiment—one of her favorite responses was from the creator behindMelonKing, who called this period of rediscovery the“web revival”, a nod to the American Folk Revival. You can find some of these interviewsup in full on Blog Therapy, and make sure to check out the latest one :)
On the indie web and dérive as resistance

Ever since I started to actively try to improve my so-called “social media addiction”, I’ve just naturally made this move from subconscious to conscious awareness of the larger issue at hand, and in turn, have organically found myself in  some corners of the indie web by drifting from link to link. I was and am still active on mainstream social media because I couldn’t look past the fact that, despite its many flaws, it could be used for good, and ultimately, that was how it was possible for me to build this space.

That’s why it actually felt refreshing when Alex pushed back on the concept of social media addiction. When I brought it up, she mentioned she finds calling it an addiction perpetuates misconceptions about the gap it’s attempting to fill—especially for young people. She then posed the following questions:
What if it’s actually an imperfect solution to a much bigger problem?
What if we’re chronically on social media not because of addiction, but because that’s where everything and everybody is?
She points out how these platforms monopolize and control our lives to the point that the real world is online now—for most, this means just a few apps run by the same three mega corporations.

However, she also brought up the point I mentioned earlier on, reframing our view of social media to foster community and resistance by highlighting all the fundraisers, protests, garage sales, buy-nothing groups, and marches organized via Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, etc.

To actually drive significant change by using social media as a powerful tool of connection and resistance, though, we must understand at a large scale that the loss of agency and attention are just the tip of the iceberg—mere symptoms of a much more complex underlying issue. Alex illustrates this very clearly: “Consider that when an authoritarian regime wants to hide what it’s doing to their own people, they cut off internet access. Because social media can be an impartial tool for truth-telling and democracy.”

Rather than dwelling on the impending doom that we’ve gotten used to, what if we stop waiting for someone else to do something about it and start with what we can actually control?That’s why I wanted to explore dérive in the first place, but without an infrastructure, that wouldn’t be possible. The indie web and the web revival at large IS the infrastructure—it’s “an online world that’s way more diverse, much broader, more privacy-friendly, more welcoming to creativity, self-expression, and autonomous exploration.”
On accessibility and getting started
When I asked Alex about the shifts she believed need to happen to actually make the indie web more accessible, and a genuine alternative to doomscrolling, here’s what she answered:

Practical: “Many non-technical people are intimidated by the idea of building and hosting your own site. We need to build awareness about the existing options (some of which I mentioned earlier on) and work to create newer, better ones. Starting a site or blog shouldn’t be any more complicated than getting Gmail set up.”

Psychological: “We need to move away from passive consumption to (pro)active discovery and outreach. Many of us just aren’t used to that anymore. On mainstream platforms, you don’t need to go looking for your interests, your people; the algorithm narrows down the content you get until it’s just right. This is convenient, but it also dulls curiosity and takes away our intellectual autonomy, our ability to discern about the content we consume”

Personal: “It’s reclaiming our curiosity, our interests, and our desires. This requires a level of autonomy that’s incompatible with mindless doomscrolling. If you want to make the most of the indie web, you need to get back in touch with your interests, forget about being cringe, and follow your curiosity (online and off). There’s a powerful resistance here and I think a lot of us would benefit from it.”

Even though I was already familiar with some places on the indie web, a few of which I’ve shared with you previously, I had only scratched the surface. I wanted to show you how I practice what I preach by fully immersing myself in these spaces through the frameworks that resonate with me: curiosity-led research and consumption turning into creation. Regardless of all the challenges I faced during this project and the shifts that still need to happen—both personally and at large—it was more than worth it and I’m glad that I picked up a new hobby in the process.

I truly want the internet to feel alive and expressive rather than oppressive for more than just a few. My goal is always to make these shifts more accessible and achievable for as many people as possible, so I hope this piece tips the scales, even if just a little, toward a more creative, inclusive, curious, and indie future.
Some closing words from Alex on how the indie web has impacted her life:

I’m excited about being online again. Working in media for almost a decade now, I got pretty cynical about what’s out there. Sure, there was my work as an editor, and that was mostly fun (especially on the digital culture news desk), but the internet seemed to be shrinking.

But getting into the indie web, the online world seemed togrowagain. I think a good comparison is hopping off the guided tour to get lost down random side streets and alleys instead. Wandering based on curiosity and intuition rather than being led by the hand to my next destination.
It’s really put me back in touch with my interests. Now, when I’m on Instagram for too long, I start to feel a bit dead inside and log off to play with my dog or something.
A big thank you to Alex for agreeing to be a part of this very special project, everyone please go send her some love!
Sending love to all of you who made it all the way here, I’m excited to share more upcoming collaborations and I hope you have lots of fun on your own digital dérive :)
Writing to you from my bed with my emotional support fidget egg,
Here are some ways to contribute to this community in any way you can꩜
If you have any cool websites you’d like to share with me, or just want to say hi and have a chat, just reply to this email, I’d love to hear from you <3

raw_article:
Welcome to this week’s media roundup! Haven’t done one of these in a while. I had a very challenging start to the year, so my inner perfectionist had to learn to let go a little and prioritize myself. The health issues that I am going through have forced me to reassess my entire life and how I want to navigate it, which is something I’d like to share with you at some point, because I know that I am not alone in this. Being so motivated to experience life, but being held back by the limits of my body has been an ongoing battle for me, but I think that sharing a little bit of it with you will help us feel less alone <3

Reading has been one of my anchors, and especially during these turbulent times—both personally and globally—reading and writing are as political as ever. So I hope that these pieces inspire you as much as they’ve inspired me to hold on to hope in our humanity :)

Sithara's Newslettara
What has the "Writer Aesthetic" cost us?
I’m about 60K words into the (hopefully) last draft of my novel manuscript. It’s an ostensibly pretty stupid fantasy novel, inspired by an adolescence spent idolising Terry Pratchett and an adulthood spent watching The Princess Bride and Stardust whenever I’m sick. Not exactly Joycean modernism. And yet I must’ve said “manuscript” too close to an iPhone mic this Sunday, because when I opened Reels, I was flooded by post after post of “writing aesthetic” content…
Read more
3 months ago · 733 likes · 75 comments · Sithara Ranasinghe

Essay arguing that the cultural obsession with the idealized aesthetic of being a writer—particularly within social media—is marketed as a lifestyle rather than a vocation, commodifying identity by diluting literary history through algorithm-driven engagement.

Who gets to call themselves a writer? Is being a writer something intrinsic or extrinsic to our identity? These are questions that have been subtly jabbing at me as I figure out what I want to do with my life, but the one question that has pierced its way through is: Why do I write? In an attempt to answer this question, I’ve gone as far back in my family lineage as I could—hoping to find the answer come down to simple genetics.

I was lucky enough to be able to spend my mom’s birthday with her after many years, so given the special occasion, the whole family—my mom, my dad, my cat, and I—got comfortable in my parents’ king-sized bed and watched their wedding video for the very first time after 25 years, which they had never watched either (I promise this sudden deviation in my quest to find an answer is getting somewhere). After the ceremony, to kick off the celebration of the newlyweds, my grandpa gave a beautiful speech, which he concluded by dedicating a poem to my parents titled Estar Enamorado—or The Essence of Being in Love—by Francisco Luis Bernárdez, and at the very end he added a couple of verses of his own authorship. When I noticed that his voice made ripples through the room, emotions tangible and buzzing, both in the one from a quarter of a century ago and in the one where my parents were looking back at themselves with a few extra wrinkles on their faces, answers began rushing into me.

Circling back to the essay, there seems to be an increasingly blurred line between the romanticization and the aestheticization of the writer. The way I see it, romanticizing means becoming an alchemist of the mundane—of the often grueling process of bringing something forth. On its own, however, aestheticizing is merely a fragile shell: a homogenized and diluted image of what the naked eye, often trained by social media, has been led to believe the writer looks like—easily shattered, revealing nothing of substance inside.

My answer isn’t set in stone, and it probably never will be, but I do know why I write and why I think I’m finally comfortable calling myself a writer. I saw it in my mom’s eyes when she looked at my grandpa reciting the poem. Sure, genetics may play a role, but most of all, both he and I want to give shape to the abstract feelings from within through the shared language of words, building webs of connection. The shell that I put on display doesn’t show a fixed image; I’d like to believe it’s see-through—at least to the ones who care enough to look. You’ll see a glimpse into my soul: a whirlwind of books, loss, films, love, songs, memories, poems, heartbreak, places, shame, and all the fragments I’ve picked up throughout my life. I write to weave them all together in an attempt to build connections, which I then share in hopes of becoming a fragment connected to someone else’s self.

Wuthering Heights Was Not a Swoony Romance. Then Hollywood Got Involved on Time Magazine

This section of today’s roundup is sponsored by Ground News. As always, I only recommend things I genuinely think you’ll enjoy as much as I do :)

Article arguing that Wuthering Heights is not a love story by outlining what the adaptation missed and featuring experts’ opinions, highlighting why Fennel’s overlooking and skipping essential plot points and characters surrounding themes of violence, abuse, and oppression is problematic.

I read Wuthering Heights last year, shortly before the casting of this film was announced. With the story fresh in my mind, I was in complete disbelief regarding these choices; however, I tried to remain open-minded until further information was announced. This was never a love story to me; it was a story about revenge, violence, obsession, and the subversion of the power dynamics of oppression. It’s safe to say that after watching the trailer and seeing an erotically bizarre representation of this politically charged story, I was left with a knot in my stomach.

I still haven’t watched the film, but as a lover of both cinema and literature, I needed to understand the general sentiment surrounding it—especially outside the echo chambers filled with people who share my exact same opinions. Whenever I feel strongly about something, as part of my media literacy strategies, I make sure to step back and look at the bigger picture to construct an informed opinion. In a media landscape where everyone has something to say, this isn’t always easy, so tools like Ground News become incredibly useful to navigate it.

Essentially, Ground News processes thousands of articles daily and allows us to find them all in one place, which makes it ideal to be able to compare what is being reported on a story across sources. Additionally, the factuality and ownership features ease the process of this assessment in order to find the sources I want to engage with.

By looking at the bigger picture through this lens, I noticed how most outlets reactively focused on either the most obvious flaws or simply ignored the weight of the themes of the novel entirely. I was able to sort through all of the sources with ease, allowing me to ground my stance in the broader critical landscape and find the necessary context to more objectively understand the importance of these departures, which is done brilliantly in the article I’m sharing with you today.

Conversation between John Berger—art critic, novelist, painter, and poet—and Susan Sontag—writer and critic—where they discuss storytelling, from its origin in the spoken word, to the role and experience of the contemporary author.

I watched this video for the first time around a year ago. It was included on the first TikTok I posted sharing the “media I consumed this week instead of doomscrolling.” Its virality created a butterfly effect that changed the course of my life. It seems fitting that this conversation between these two intellectual giants is about storytelling because, as I’ve shared with you before, the seed that has now bloomed into finding my purpose was planted during a talk by the author Collum McCan, in which he spoke about the importance of telling stories.

Savoring this full-circle moment, I was able to observe the growth between the version of myself that watched it for the first time and the one typing these words. Before, I felt like a simple observer. I was introduced to new concepts, books, authors, and movements, which I wrote down to explore later, much like a student taking notes in a lecture. This time around, though, I felt like an active participant in the conversation. I was confident enough in my knowledge and perspective to form my own opinions, agreeing or disagreeing with their arguments as they did with each other.

I was going to condense all of my notes and thoughts about my stance on their discussion while contextualizing and expanding on these ideas, given the evolution of storytelling and their models in contemporary times. I might write a separate piece on this at some point, but right now, I believe it’s far more important to highlight the importance of continuous learning to be able to construct our points of view. Being able to define, discern, and communicate our beliefs and opinions, rather than accepting what we are told as truth, is one of our greatest tools of opposition.

Reading as Resistance Perfectly Imperfect

Recommendation list created by me, where I share things to do instead of doomscrolling. In this occasion, I share a some of the ways in which I have been inspired to be resilient, curious, and consistent throughout my journey as a reader <3

Here is the intro to this installment:

Reading is one of the most accessible, yet impactful forms of resistance because, as we’ve heard a million times, knowledge is power. Taking this sentiment further, though, getting to know ourselves and determining our values, what we stand for, and building hopeful critical thinking skills is even more impactful because knowledge is futile unless we actually take the time to think. I could simply write a reading list of books that have radicalised me, but that would defeat the point that I’m getting at. Instead, I’ll be sharing some of the ways in which I have built, expanded, and persevered throughout my reading journey.

Podcast featuring a conversation between On Being host Krista Tippett and Maria Popova, writer and creator of The Marginalian—previously known as Brain Pickings—where they discuss the importance of literature, sharing the human experience through digital means, spiritual fulfillment, and more.

Every single time I listen to an episode of On Being, I know something inside me will shift, this movement allowing for something to finally fall into place. This time around, though, I’d say that this wasn’t just a shift, but rather a whole earthquake. As I began exploring the ground from which I gathered all of my inspiration and resources in this journey, I inevitably came across Maria Popova.

While I was listening to this conversation, I couldn’t help but feel like so many of the unspoken drivers of my devotion toward life and the work I love sharing with you on here were being put into words. It can feel somewhat reductive when I try to explain what this publication is about, because yes, it’s about engaging with media and the digital world more mindfully, but at its core, this project is—in Popovas’ words—how, through these methods, we can build an intellectual life for spiritual survival.

Typing for Love or Money—The Hidden Women’s Labor behind Modern Literary Masterpieces on The Public Domain Review

Essay analyzing the history and origins of typewriting by highlighting the lives of various women who worked as typists—often for their husbands—becoming essential contributors in some of the most important works of modern literature.

As I was reading my way through this piece, I remembered this note I posted a while ago on here. I got reminded of the fact that my perception of women’s unseen labor and work is so unfortunately narrow, and that, regardless of the losses that have been suffered, sometimes it’s just a matter of looking hard enough to see what does remain.

I was rewatching Peaky Blinders recently, and if you’re familiar with it, you know that Lizzie—who was previously a prostitute—was able to abandon that brutal life when she decided to learn how to type and was hired for it. Without spoiling too much, she eventually became the secretary (and perhaps a bit more than that) of the main character; the bad and scary Thomas Shelby. She was undoubtedly keeping everything together; she wasn’t just a typist, she was the backbone of both the family business and the emotional support of the not-so-scary Tommy Shelby.

In a way, this fictional story does illustrate the reality that the records left of and by the women we learn about in this piece: Theodora Bosanquet, Véra Nabokov, and Valerie Eliot. These outstanding women were not only underecognized for their literary labor but also for their emotional labor, which is something that is still heavily undervalued to this day. The recognition of typists’ work allows us to expand our perception of authorship, collaboration, and the imbalance of gendered power dynamics throughout literary history.

Hope you enjoyed these pieces and my thoughts on them, it felt good to be able to write like this again. If you enjoyed this post, consider becoming a paid subscriber, your support allows me to keep writing and to grow our community of learners :)

-Mapu

As I was reading my way through this piece, I remembered this note I posted a while ago on here. I got reminded of the fact that my perception of women’s unseen labor and work is so unfortunately narrow, and that, regardless of the losses that have been suffered, sometimes it’s just a matter of looking hard enough to see what does remain.

I was rewatchingPeaky Blindersrecently, and if you’re familiar with it, you know that Lizzie—who was previously a prostitute—was able to abandon that brutal life when she decided to learn how to type and was hired for it. Without spoiling too much, she eventually became the secretary (and perhaps a bit more than that) of the main character; the bad and scary Thomas Shelby. She was undoubtedly keeping everything together; she wasn’t just a typist, she was the backbone of both the family business and the emotional support of the not-so-scary Tommy Shelby.

In a way, this fictional story does illustrate the reality that the records left of and by the women we learn about in this piece: Theodora Bosanquet, Véra Nabokov, and Valerie Eliot. These outstanding women were not only underecognized for their literary labor but also for their emotional labor, which is something that is still heavily undervalued to this day. The recognition of typists’ work allows us to expand our perception of authorship, collaboration, and the imbalance of gendered power dynamics throughout literary history.

Hope you enjoyed these pieces and my thoughts on them, it felt good to be able to write like this again. If you enjoyed this post, consider becoming a paid subscriber, your support allows me to keep writing and to grow our community of learners :)
-Mapu

subtitle: Notes on censorship, deepfakes, creativity, education, mediocrity, political writing, and more.
author: mapuc
raw_article:
Welcome to this week’s media roundup! I’ve been really struggling with my health lately, pretty much everything in my life has taken an abrupt pause, but a phrase that I’ve heard bouncing around lately is that action is the antidote to despair, so I’m holding on to that. Even though I still need to take things slowly, these pieces have provided me with a much-needed push toward creation, so I really hope they can do the same for you <3

I recently shared some of my latest obsessions and little pieces of myself over on ! If you would like to check it out you can find it here :)

Punk, Power, and Censorship from East Berlin to Gaza on Byline

Essay arguing that resisting censorship and authoritarianism can be done by staying loud and present through coordinated action, protests, concerts, and art—using KvU’s history in East Berlin to Palestinian art and activism as examples through the times.

I open my feed, I see yet another atrocity through the pixels of my screen, I scroll, and I scroll, and I scroll as I attempt to self-soothe to forget the day’s brutality, I do it again the next day—it seems to be a closed loop, it’s not though. Forgetting something doesn’t mean it didn’t happen, and not seeing something in the first place doesn’t mean it didn’t happen either, which is something we must remember in this age of modern censorship and exploitative algorithms.

I hate that social media has created a space where I can look at someone being brutally murdered and look at some AI slop within the span of a minute. I hate that it has made us move on from wars and genocides like they’re trends. I hate that I know it is being weaponized to silence the voices that go against certain agendas. What to do with all of this hate, then? It is totally futile if we keep it to ourselves, so even if it’s all we can do with it, let’s make sure to let it be known.

Behind Grok’s mass digital undressing lies an ‘unsurprising’ cohort of ‘men eager to violate women’ on ABC Australia

This section of today’s roundup is sponsored by Ground News. As always, I only recommend things I genuinely think you’ll enjoy as much as I do :)

Article reporting on the concerning use of the Grok chatbot to digitally remove women’s and children’s clothes, and the measures that are being taken by X to prevent it after international backlash.

Recently, I heard someone say that the sexualization of women is only appealing as long as it’s non-consensual, which I find to be perfectly illustrated here. I don’t think I could ever possibly find the words to describe the disgust and rage that this makes me feel, It’s so disturbing to witness how deepfakes are becoming a such a serious threat to privacy, image rights, and consent—especially with the lack of laws and regulations around AI.

I first came across this phenomenon on social media, listening to the testimonies of the women who have been victims of this vicious transgression. I was immediately appalled by this and decided to investigate further to see what was being done about it. In my journey of improving my media literacy, something that has become of utmost importance to me is to understand the context behind the information that I’m consuming—this being both how factual the source is and who’s behind it, as well as cross-checking with a range of sources to avoid echo chambers and find the one most aligned with what I seek. This is a really complex task so that’s why tools like Ground News become incredibly useful to navigate the media landscape.

Essentially, Ground News processes thousands of articles daily and allows us to find them all in one place, which makes it ideal to be able to compare what is being reported on a story across sources. Additionally, the factuality and ownership features eases the process of this assessment in order to find the sources I want to engage with.

When I came across this story, it was important to me to have the most fact-based understanding of the measures being taken by not just X, and multiple governments, but also how this fits in the broader landscape of gender-based violence and oppression, which through Ground News features, led me to find this specific article.

Ground News is entirely subscriber-supported, just like I am. If you’re interested, you can subscribe through the link at groundnews.com/mapu or click below to get 40% off the unlimited Vantage plan, which comes out to $5 a month—the same one that I use :)

Subscribe to Ground News

Podcast analyzing the role of creativity as an essential, yet conflicting element of our identity, consciousness, purpose, and expectations, exploring how creative power can become expansive or destructive.

Sometimes I wonder how it would feel to go about life as some sort of finance bro who can’t perceive themselves as creative. I’d only read scammy self-help books that teach me how to manipulate people and make money. I’d realize empathy is a thing after trying shrooms, and go around saying I had an ego death to women who came to that same realization at 12. I’d never question who I am because everything around me has been built so that I’d never have to. However, once I’m done wondering, I’m left with a feeling of disgust because even though ignorance seems like bliss in this oversimplified and satirical scenario, I would never trade the agony of creation for the sake of certainty.

This podcast really stirred something in me; it made me realize that when I create, I’m having a conversation with myself. In this figurative conversation, I ask questions that may not have an answer, yet I reply with the closest thing that resembles the truth—a truth of selfhood, rather than of fact. These conversations can be beautiful and simple, but more often than not, they end up killing versions of ourselves that must rot so that new ones can grow from the ground where they were buried. Creating is the ambivalent act of becoming over and over again.

If you value the time and care that goes into my work and want to join a community of learners, consider becoming a paid subscriber <3

Imaginology on Aeon

Essay arguing that the current state of education is rooted in polarization between scientific and humanistic knowledge, and that a model of “Imagination Studies” where imagination is the engine of cognition, can bridge this gap through integration.

There once was a time when artists and scientists were one and the same, when empirical science was inextricably linked with philosophical enquiry. Fast forward to maybe ten years ago, when I was in middle school; I absolutely excelled in art and English classes, and one very special teacher recognized that and made me feel smart, perhaps for the first time ever. She realized that my mind worked a little differently and made sure to leverage that to help me become an overall better student—even in the classes I didn’t care for much.

Looking back, that very short-lived year was integral to my way of understanding the world. I know that now, but back in high school, I forgot all about it and let myself be convinced that I was at the very bottom of the academic hierarchy. I thought I wasn’t smart just because I couldn’t grasp math and science like I did philosophy and literature. Having made it out the other side and slowly but surely becoming interested in these so-called STEM subjects, the only reason I can even attempt to understand them now is because of all of the frameworks of understanding that my beloved humanities have given me. Perhaps if we collectively moved toward this imaginative way of learning, the future could be a little brighter.

Under the Weather on Perfectly Imperfect

Recommendation list created by me, where I share things to do instead of doomscrolling such as activities, books, shows, films, fun corners of the internet, and more :)

Here is the intro to this week’s installment:

I have been sick for a couple of weeks now, and whenever this happens, I know I’m particularly prone to spending hours mindlessly scrolling in the darkness of my room. Losing a sense of normalcy can be distressing—especially as a result of our bodies not being able to keep up with the speed at which the world is spinning. In the process of recovery, I know the last thing I should do is put too much pressure on myself to do the most, so this week, I’ll share with you a few fun things I’ve been doing to take my mind off things and feel a little better.

Video essay exploring what it means to be mediocre in the modern world, and how the lack of agency over our individual search for significance in our lives results in persistent and prolonged fear of mediocrity.

If you were to die tomorrow, would you be satisfied with what you did with your life? Not to send you into the same existential spiral I went down on, but essentially, this is the question that we must ask ourselves when we explore our fear of mediocrity. I suppose each one of us envisions something different, but something was made clear to me when I attempted to answer this question. I’ve been a chronic perfectionist my whole life, but I realized that even though, on paper, so much of what I’ve done is everything but mediocre, this “perfection” serves everyone but myself. Either way, regardless of how different our answers may be, I think it’s safe to say that conformity would be one common regret.

In the garden of forking paths, where every choice we make splits life into different realities, I like to approach every decision as a path toward meeting the most individuated version of myself, as far away as possible from the one who chose to conform—the one I would deem mediocre. In the midst of this Borgesian parable, I know I’m likely to land somewhere in between the two, but I like to think that the gut feeling that I know I should trust is this true version of myself leading me her way.

culture shock
"to make revolution irresistible"
Read more
2 years ago · 827 likes · 21 comments · Pranay Somayajula

Essay arguing that there is a divide between literature and revolutionary politics, rooted in the lack of literary value that is given to political writing, given that it doesn’t abide by the bourgeois ideals of the aesthetic over the polemic.

“in our failure to acknowledge the true literary potential of political writing, we reinforce literature’s role as a tool for the preservation of elite interests in bourgeois society—suitable, perhaps, as an avenue for escapist fantasizing and the disciplining of would-be revolutionaries, but never as a battlefield for any meaningful form of direct political contestation or struggle.”

This quote stood out to me as I was reading this essay—as I hope all of you do—and it made me think of the mythologized version of a revolution that has been constructed by fictional stories. Think of all of these dystopian sci-fi books that have been adopted for the big screen such as Dune, The Hunger Games, The Maze Runner, Divergent, etc. It seems to be okay to send political messages as long as they’re not overtly political and masked by entertainment because, let’s be honest, media literacy rates aren’t high enough for people to actually get the message.

The core of the essay is that, as writers, we must make the revolution irresistible by ripping off the embellished veil of literary noise to uncover the political messages and let them be loud and clear. I’m taking it upon myself to reflect on what this means for me as a writer, but also as a reader, because how am I supposed to get the message if I’m not even listening—I hope you get to do the same.

title: Media To Consume This Week Instead of Doomscrolling - Entering the New Year
subtitle: Notes on performative creativity, The New Years, curiosity, analog activities, awe, the old internet, and more.
author: mapuc
raw_article:
Hello everyone and welcome to the first media roundup of the year! Over the very few days that have passed since the beginning of 2026, I already get the sense that it’s going to be quite a long year. Seeing this stark and dystopian global landscape and feeling powerless within it is something I’m sure we’re all familiar with, but I refuse to do nothing about it. One of the most powerful tools that every single one of us has is creativity because art and expression are forms of resistance. This year I’m making sure to take as much advantage of it as I possibly can, and I hope today’s pieces inspire you to do the same <3

PSA in honor of having 400k of you here, I’m hosting a live this Sunday to answer some of your questions and to share a bit more about myself and how this publication came to be! I really hope to see you there :) You can add it to your calendar and find more info about it here.

Subscribe now

Video essay breaking down the performance of creativity and how the rise of the multi-creative persona has resulted in the aestheticization of craft without action.

eing in fashion school has allowed me to encounter many fascinating characters. On one hand, I’ve been able to experience the minds of some incredibly brilliant creative individuals, people whose art and message are deeply embodied. On the other hand, I’ve also met plenty of self-proclaimed creatives; they usually have the most aesthetically pleasing Instagrams, but the most superficial art and personalities.

We exist in a world filtered through idealization, it’s partly because of social media, of course, but I also think it’s a collective coping mechanism to be able to think about the future without falling into total nihilism—aspiration is often the only thing keeping us going. The problem comes when we only aspire to the ends without aspiring to the means. In this case of wanting to be a multi-creative individual, so many fall in love with a skewed glamorized idea of what this may be, without thinking about what it entails. I think a lot of people want to be creatives without having to create—to just be without having to become.

The New Years on MUBI

The New Years is a Spanish series directed by Goya-winner Rodrigo Sorogoyen, spanning ten consecutive New Year’s Eves. It follows the dramatic romance of Ana and Óscar, exploring how their relationship evolves as they grow together and apart—how people change, drift, reconnect, and mature as the years go by.

The New Year has always stirred conflicting feelings within me; I am faced not only with the year that has passed but the year that is about to come. I reflect upon the things I’ve accomplished, resolutions that weren’t met, the people I’ve left behind, and those I hope will be with me this time next year. There’s a mystical quality to this day where all veils seem to dissipate, forcing you to face uncomfortable truths within yourself and those around you. This intimate, vulnerable feeling stared right back at me as I watched Ana and Óscar through the New Years that went by.

Something I’ve struggled with my whole life is letting people in long enough for them to actually see me evolve into different versions of myself. Perhaps we simply grow in different directions, or perhaps we aren’t willing to face the discomfort that comes when—regardless of that distance—you still want to keep them in your life. As I saw glimpses of Ana and Óscar’s relationship, it reminded me of how certain people feel like recurring dreams, manifesting periodically—sometimes occasionally, other times for nights on end. They emerge from a repressed part of the subconscious because they represent something that must be faced.

My perception of romance has always been skewed, carefully constructed through the polarizing ends of a spectrum: it is always either perfection or destruction. Yet, something as complex as love is far more nuanced. As much as I love cinema, it’s not always easy for me to get behind romances; so much of the genre feels detached, plastic, and shallow. Watching this series was the antithesis of that—it was real, awkward, passionate, tender, and imperfect. Seeing these deeply human characters navigating the familiar settings of Spain—as nights turned to days and days turned to years—reminded me of a simple truth. To find something real, we must accept that neither we, nor the ones we love, will ever stay the same.

Episodes 1–8 of The New Years are now streaming on MUBI. Get 30 days free at mubi.com/mapu to watch the series and explore a carefully curated selection of films & series that will stay with you long after the credits roll :)

Available in 🇺🇸🇨🇦🇬🇧🇮🇪🇦🇺🇳🇱🇹🇷🇮🇳🇦🇷🇧🇷🇨🇱🇨🇴

Why are we curious about some things and indifferent to others? on Psyche

Article dissecting how and why some things spark our curiosity more than others—through the lens of both cognitive science and philosophy—and exploring how we may be able to expand its scope.

I like to think of curiosity as a muscle, one that must be exercised, strengthened, and nourished. Now, if we think of the process of learning as a workout, muscles must be pushed to their limits if we want them to heal to be even bigger and stronger, which can only be sustained through consistency, but also recovery. As much as this process might hurt or as uncomfortable as it may be, if you find what makes you feel good, it’s endlessly rewarding.

Instant gratification, intellectual laziness, and decision fatigue have been the death of our curiosity drive—when curiosity is dormant, life becomes static, dull, and repetitive. The way I picture this is like the other mother’s world in Coraline; at first, it seems like a utopia, absolute comfort, absolute beauty, and absolute convenience. However, soon enough, reality starts to make its way through the cracks, eventually unveiling and freezing this state of absolute decay. Being curious in the real world isn’t always comfortable, convenient, or beautiful, but this friction that happens in the process is the one that creates the spark to propel you into growth.

52 analog activities for every week of 2026

4 months ago · 4525 likes · 62 comments · Madalin

Comprehensive list full of creative and whimsical analog activities for 2026 ranging from crafts, experiences, reading and writing prompts, games and more.

It seems like analog is the new buzz word, I’ve seen it slowly but surely creep its way into the cultural zeitgeist. When words become too repetitive within the current discourse, I tend to hold a slight aversion to them, mostly because people use them too liberally and inaccurately. I hadn’t given much thought to this word until one of my favorite fashion publications published an issue titled “Analog” which I really enjoyed. With the concept now being internalized my aversion is now gone, because even if it feels repetitive, analog is exactly what we need right now—and this list is a truly wonderful place to start.

Here are some of the activities from the list I’m excited to get to do:

  • Making a recipe book

  • Learning how to make a lino cut stamp

  • Replacing buttons on old clothes

  • Re-reading your favorite childhood books

Human Connection Over Algorithmic Disconnection on Perfectly Imperfect

Recommendation list created by me, where I share things to do instead of doomscrolling such as activities, books, shows, films, fun corners of the internet, and more :)

Here is the intro to this week’s installment:

I don’t know about you, but personally, I want my 2026 to feel like 2016. There is a fervent collective nostalgia for that year we experienced a decade ago, and as the trend cycle goes, the 2000s are out, and the 2010s are in. It’s not like I care about the trend cycle too much, but I have definitely noticed how it’s manifesting on the internet, and I would be lying if I said I wasn’t thrilled about it.

With the current state of the world, it feels almost inevitable to feel disconnected; AI slop everywhere, endless feeds with addictive algorithms, an unstable global landscape, loneliness rates at historic highs, and, honestly, the list could go on and on. I’m not saying that a few years ago things were perfect. Still, at least we had a better sense of connection, so I’m taking it into my own hands to prioritize human connection over algorithmic disconnection. Below, you’ll find some of the ways I find and enjoy human recommendations.

Podcast featuring a conversation with a psychologist who has specialized in what awe does to our minds and bodies, where they explore ways in which we can improve our lives by slowing down and looking intently at the world around us.

When was the last time you felt a true sense of awe, when you were overcome with emotion as you experienced something sublime? It took me a second to think about it myself—which I think speaks to the fact that I need to slow down a little—but it was actually yesterday as I watched the sunset. Sunsets at home are quite rare because the sun often just hides behind the mountains without announcing itself, but every now and then, when the clouds are floating just right, we get the most beautiful show of colors in the sky.

For a little context, I was back home in Bogota, and my dad was driving us back home for the last time before I had to travel back to Italy to finish my final semester of uni. I’ve been really restless about coming back; not only am I not going to be home or see my family for six months and enter an entirely new stage of my life, but I’ve had a few health complications, which I’m terrified to face all alone. All this to say that I’ve been having a lot of confusing and bittersweet feelings lately, ones that make life make absolutely no sense. However, the wonderful thing about experiencing awe is that at least for a brief moment, everything seems to make sense.
