body.content: Hello everyone and welcome to the first media roundup of the year! Over the very few days that have passed since the beginning of 2026, I already get the sense that it’s going to be quite a long year. Seeing this stark and dystopian global landscape and feeling powerless within it is something I’m sure we’re all familiar with, but I refuse to do nothing about it. One of the most powerful tools that every single one of us has is creativity because art and expression are forms of resistance. This year I’m making sure to take as much advantage of it as I possibly can, and I hope today’s pieces inspire you to do the same <3

PSA in honor of having 400k of you here, I’m hosting a live this Sunday to answer some of your questions and to share a bit more about myself and how this publication came to be! I really hope to see you there :)You can add it to your calendar and find more info about it here.
Video essay breaking down the performance of creativity and how the rise of the multi-creative persona has resulted in the aestheticization of craft without action.

eing in fashion school has allowed me to encounter many fascinating characters. On one hand, I’ve been able to experience the minds of some incredibly brilliant creative individuals, people whose art and message are deeply embodied. On the other hand, I’ve also met plenty of self-proclaimed creatives; they usually have the most aesthetically pleasing Instagrams, but the most superficial art and personalities.

We exist in a world filtered through idealization, it’s partly because of social media, of course, but I also think it’s a collective coping mechanism to be able to think about the future without falling into total nihilism—aspiration is often the only thing keeping us going. The problem comes when we only aspire to the ends without aspiring to the means. In this case of wanting to be a multi-creative individual, so many fall in love with a skewed glamorized idea of what this may be, without thinking about what it entails. I think a lot of people want to be creatives without having to create—to just be without having to become.
The New Years on MUBI

The New Yearsis a Spanish series directed by Goya-winner Rodrigo Sorogoyen, spanning ten consecutive New Year’s Eves. It follows the dramatic romance of Ana and Óscar, exploring how their relationship evolves as they grow together and apart—how people change, drift, reconnect, and mature as the years go by.

The New Year has always stirred conflicting feelings within me; I am faced not only with the year that has passed but the year that is about to come. I reflect upon the things I’ve accomplished, resolutions that weren’t met, the people I’ve left behind, and those I hope will be with me this time next year. There’s a mystical quality to this day where all veils seem to dissipate, forcing you to face uncomfortable truths within yourself and those around you. This intimate, vulnerable feeling stared right back at me as I watched Ana and Óscar through the New Years that went by.

Something I’ve struggled with my whole life is letting people in long enough for them to actually see me evolve into different versions of myself. Perhaps we simply grow in different directions, or perhaps we aren’t willing to face the discomfort that comes when—regardless of that distance—you still want to keep them in your life. As I saw glimpses of Ana and Óscar’s relationship, it reminded me of how certain people feel like recurring dreams, manifesting periodically—sometimes occasionally, other times for nights on end. They emerge from a repressed part of the subconscious because they represent something that must be faced.

My perception of romance has always been skewed, carefully constructed through the polarizing ends of a spectrum: it is always either perfection or destruction. Yet, something as complex as love is far more nuanced. As much as I love cinema, it’s not always easy for me to get behind romances; so much of the genre feels detached, plastic, and shallow. Watching this series was the antithesis of that—it was real, awkward, passionate, tender, and imperfect. Seeing these deeply human characters navigating the familiar settings of Spain—as nights turned to days and days turned to years—reminded me of a simple truth. To find something real, we must accept that neither we, nor the ones we love, will ever stay the same.

Episodes 1–8 of The New Years are now streaming on MUBI. Get 30 days free atmubi.com/maputo watch the series and explore a carefully curated selection of films & series that will stay with you long after the credits roll :)
Why are we curious about some things and indifferent to others? on Psyche
Article dissecting how and why some things spark our curiosity more than others—through the lens of both cognitive science and philosophy—and exploring how we may be able to expand its scope.

I like to think of curiosity as a muscle, one that must be exercised, strengthened, and nourished. Now, if we think of the process of learning as a workout, muscles must be pushed to their limits if we want them to heal to be even bigger and stronger, which can only be sustained through consistency, but also recovery. As much as this process might hurt or as uncomfortable as it may be, if you find what makes you feel good, it’s endlessly rewarding.

Instant gratification, intellectual laziness, and decision fatigue have been the death of our curiosity drive—when curiosity is dormant, life becomes static, dull, and repetitive. The way I picture this is like the other mother’s world in Coraline; at first, it seems like a utopia, absolute comfort, absolute beauty, and absolute convenience. However, soon enough, reality starts to make its way through the cracks, eventually unveiling and freezing this state of absolute decay. Being curious in the real world isn’t always comfortable, convenient, or beautiful, but this friction that happens in the process is the one that creates the spark to propel you into growth.

title: New Year’s Absolutions
subtitle: The antithesis of New Year’s resolutions that actually transformed my life
author: mapuc
raw_article:
I have always been a daydreamer—especially when I was younger—conceptualizing idealized realities and impossible versions of myself. I found refuge in my mind, living vicariously through these imagined versions; these dreamscapes provided a sense of freedom whenever I felt stuck. In a way, whenever I used to write my New Year’s resolutions, I did the exact same thing—except I couldn’t tell that these were fantasies at all.

Each new year that passes brings on a sense of impending doom for me. I feel completely frozen as I overthink every little detail of the year that has passed and the one ahead—dissecting and projecting growth and grief alike. This feeling of inertia is a two-way street, though. If I stop abruptly, whiplash is sure to follow. But if I suddenly speed up to try to fast-track change, I’ll always chase it but never reach it. Without going into too much detail, that was the dilemma I faced this New Year: how do I actually navigate this sudden change? I swiftly concluded that I don’t, because there is no sudden change, because change is slow but constant, rather than conditionally self-imposed.

I keep seeing people creating guides to have a “rebrand” in 2026. I see elaborate plans for personal curriculums, absurd and rigid metrics to be reached across all areas of life—be that reading, working out, or personal habits—and even shopping lists that are marketing change as something you can buy. I can’t bring myself to be truly critical of people indulging in these, though, because this was me not that long ago.

Subscribe now

I used to compulsively plan every little detail of the upcoming year through elaborate structures, checklists, apps, wishlists, and expectations—all of which were the architecture of my so-called New Year’s resolutions. These were always a poor attempt at covering up the guilt, shame, and pain of the year that passed, but also the one that comes with failing at pretty much every single thing that I wrote in the previous year’s resolutions, sucking me into this all-consuming, vicious yearly cycle.

Last year, I got tired of this lifelong cycle of misery and decided to do things differently this time. I decided to write New Year’s absolutions rather than resolutions, and—quite ironically—it was the year that actually changed my life. By releasing all of the usual pressure and expectations, I stopped setting myself up for disappointment and created space for surprise and growth instead. I don’t have a guide to tell you how to do this exactly, given that this is a deeply personal and instinctual practice, but I will share a little bit of what mine looked like, and what lies at its core, in hopes that you can adapt it into something of your own.

I talk more about my relationship with resolutions in the last installment of Things To Do Instead of Doomscrolling. This one is titled New Year, Same Me where I share a few things that I’m bringing into my 2026 which you can find below :)

Things To Do Instead of Doomscrolling

Here are a few excerpts from last year’s absolutions, which I find capture the essence of what I’ll break down as I tell you a little bit more about how this looked like in practice throughout 2025.

“new year, same me. change comes constantly and inevitably, it comes crashing down and suffocating me under piles of rubble. evolution and renewal are welcome and desperately needed but letting go of the pressure of change makes it come authentically and righteously. i accept things as they come and let things flow through me like gentle waves.”

“ultimately holding on to my essence and detaching myself, my worth, my happiness and my love from others (that being things, places or people) is the only thing that will bring me peace.”

“pain and grief have taken leading roles in my life as of late, i am consumed by them, i don’t think it’s going away any time soon and i need to accept that. i do need to accept these feelings as much as the good ones that are always there, the ones that just seem easier to ignore, so 2025 is the year in which balance of mind brings clarity of soul.”

Absolutions are essentially a formal release from guilt, obligation, or punishment. I like to think of them as a form of acceptance and surrender, one in which we face ourselves as we are, not as what we were or want to be. Through them, we let go of the all-or-nothing mindset that results from the limitations of our expectations—we ditch the metrics, the lists, the apps, the performance, the pressure, the attachments, and the resolutions. We don’t try to build ourselves from the ground up, but rather we dig ourselves out of the rubble of change and rebuild with what’s already there.

This concept of surrendering always felt very abstract to me; to make it make sense, I understood it as giving up. I saw this as an act of failure and weakness. However, the moment I accepted that I was already consumed by these feelings, something clicked. Surrendering isn’t giving up, it’s actually the act of opening up—to both ourselves and the world around us—leaving our egos aside.

Instead of Doomscrolling is an entirely reader-supported publication. If you value the time and care that goes into my work and want to join a community of learners, consider becoming a paid subscriber <3

Here’s how I have understood and implemented the mechanics of this process:

  • Acknowledging ourselves, our feelings, and our circumstances as they are. Accepting our qualities, accomplishments, and virtues as much as our flaws and mistakes without any form of prejudice. Letting ourselves simply be.

I took a moment of deep reflection through writing, reading, meditating, and listening to others, which allowed me to accept that I was wasting so much of myself and my life stuck in a functional freeze. I realized that by wasting my days in front of a screen, doomscrolling my life away, I was scared to try to do something with all of the wonderful opportunities of life in case all of my limitations prevented me from actually taking advantage of them. This is when I decided to attempt to take mindful media consumption seriously, without creating any form of elaborate plan or setting any form of expectations.

  • Giving ourselves grace, showing ourselves kindness and compassion for said flaws and mistakes and for our reactions to them and to our circumstances.

I am a chronic perfectionist, so I knew that I had to be ready to accept when things went wrong and when I failed. Giving myself grace over spending too much time on my phone, or not reading the books I wanted to, neglecting my responsibilities, procrastinating, or treating myself badly over it was essential to not get stuck again. This also comes into play whenever I feel hopeless—especially with the deeply scary and bleak reality that we are forced to navigate right now—because it’s okay to feel scared, sad, anxious, and even depressed over the harshness of life and our inability to deal with it, because what we’re living through is unprecedented.

  • Transforming feelings into action, taking shame, joy, stress, love, pain, disappointment, hope, etc., and setting them in motion.

There is this large gap between action and feeling, or at least that’s how it is for me. I tend to over-intellectualize my feelings rather than actually living them, so they tend to get stuck in my head and leave me frozen yet again. I think recognizing how these feel on our bodies, where they come from, and ultimately embodying them is essential to create movement. I do this every time I do a media roundup and share what’s been on my mind, when I create a playlist after experiencing something new, when I write on my journal when I’m feeling grateful, when I talk to a friend after isolating myself for weeks—or when I have a really difficult New Year and I write about it to remind myself to surrender yet again and hope to help someone do the same by sharing what I’ve learned.

I am the same person that I was yesterday, but I’m not the same one I was a year ago, so why pretend that January 1st is a reset button? Time is a construct; we exist in the present, not in the past or in the future. Embracing every single day as a blank slate to open ourselves up to something new is not something reserved for the first day of the year, or of the month, or even the week—we create our own realities once we decide to surrender to being. Instead of caging ourselves in resolutions yet another year, why not try to free ourselves from them through absolutions?

Thank you for reading and I hope this year we can continue to grow together <3

- Mapu

title: Media To Consume This Week Instead of Doomscrolling: Between Comfort & Discomfort
subtitle: Notes on mysteries, feeling human, comfort, failing, social friction, self-reliance, disconnection, and more.
author: mapuc
raw_article:
Welcome everyone to this week’s media roundup! As the new year draws closer every day, I’ve been thinking about the growth that I aspire to continue experiencing next year. Whenever I feel like I’ve grown through knowledge, relationships, experiences, or reflections, a sense of comfort fills every last corner of myself. However, the hard truth is that, to get there, you must endure the discomfort of growing pains. Finding a balance between the two isn’t always easy, but this week’s pieces certainly served as a guiding light as I get ready to embark on new journeys, so I hope they can do the same for you :)

Subscribe now

Navigating the Mysteries on Emergence Magazine

Essay arguing that uncertainty must be navigated through mythmaking rather than punditry—creating stories to create meaningful engagement with mystery can allow us to navigate ambiguity by cultivating richness, wonder, and authenticity.

Being back home in my childhood bedroom feels like stepping into a portal that can bend the space-time fabric, allowing me to simultaneously see the many versions of myself that have existed within these four walls. I look at them and see how I have always had a rebellious tendency, this inherent impulse to question everything around me, but when I realized that this often brought me trouble so I tried my best to shut it down. Over the years, this repression turned into certainty—I now see how skepticism and stubbornness kept me stuck in loops of self-limiting beliefs. I was convinced that life existed at face value, that I was bound by the realities that everyone projected onto me.

The moment when all this pressure was finally released was in my high school philosophy class, where not only was questioning everything accepted, but it was also encouraged and even rewarded. A central theme explored in this class was Greek mythology. At first, I thought these stories were slightly ridiculous yet entertaining, but eventually, as I began analyzing and dissecting their symbolism, my world was infinitely expanded. Every myth—as fantastical as it was—gave me a framework to not only navigate the paradoxes within myself, but to pull me out of my head and place me in a context to embrace the mysteries in the world around me.

As I went down this memory lane, I came across my final essay for the subject; it was about the origins of Greek mythology and their social, political, and theological implications across time. I hadn’t read it in years, but looking back, it was a seed that bloomed into the way in which I understand the world around me. Through a lot of deconstruction and humility, I’ve learned to step out of dogmatic belief systems and embrace storytelling in the way I choose to write the narrative of my life. I’ll leave you with a quote from the closing paragraph of this very essay:

“I learned how valuable it truly is to immerse oneself in a story, since one never really knows what worlds of ideas and reflections could be discovered.”

Things To Do Instead of Doomscrolling - Feeling Human

Recommendation list created by me, where I share things to do instead of doomscrolling such as activities, books, shows, films, fun corners of the internet, and more :)

Here is the intro to this week’s installment:

If I could describe this past year in one word, it would be uncanny. With AI seeping through every crack of our lives, headlines that read like fever dreams, and the algorithmic flattening of identity—and honestly, I could go on—everything seems to be glazed by a layer of synthetic static. This feeling has led to a collective detachment and isolation, pushing us even deeper into our individualistic holes. That’s why it’s more important than ever to remind ourselves of the unique qualities that make us human. As a way to ground myself in this strange moment in time, I have made a conscious effort to embrace the connection with my own humanity and that of others, because that’s the only way we will remain as one.

Podcast exploring the concept of comfort and discomfort through the lens of certain philosophers across history, and how these can be understood in a contemporary context.

“Comfort lies at the edge of awareness” is a quote that kept buzzing in my head even long after I finished listening to this podcast—it led me to question the concept of awareness itself. Is it an oxymoron to say that we aren’t necessarily aware of what we are aware of? Maybe. My point is that perhaps, only once we accept the discomfort of the unknown, we allow for clarity of what is and isn’t held by the bounds of our awareness—of what the terrain of our comfort zone actually looks like.

This idea of understanding the self through edges has been fundamental to my growth over this past year. I remember I drew a couple of diagrams that helped me make sense of this concept a while ago. Funnily enough, the very evolution between one diagram and the other paralleled the one of expanding my comfort zone through an exploration into the unknown. The first diagram was drawn at the moment in which this idea came to my mind, and the second, once I had made room for uncomfortable concepts and experiences to develop the initial thought:

I’m not sure if these will make sense to anyone other than myself, but I wanted to illustrate how pushing our boundaries creates expansion. For me, this process wasn’t easy, but figuring out how to get to this edge has definitely been worth it.

How to fall in love with failing
Today, we’re talking about failure, and I’m sure that may send a shiver up your spine. It may elicit memories of business ideas left by the wayside, the C that had your considering your pure academic talent for the first time, the embarrassment of dreaming big and coming up short…

6 months ago · 406 likes · 7 comments · The Psychology of Your 20s

Substack post analyzing the fear of failure and exploring how this very feeling can be cultivated to provide growth rather than defeat or stagnancy.

You know that 3 am urge to change your life the following morning, the feeling that this time around things will be different, and you’ll actually follow through with it? I noticed that when that happens to me, it feels realistic because I am in a state of isolation. The darkness, the silence, the slight delirium of exhaustion make me forget that if I were to actually try, I would need to be seen trying—the fear of being seen trying and failing slips from my mind.

I’ve thought a lot about what failure means to me and the feelings that it raises. One of my best and worst qualities is my incessant perfectionism; this need to do something flawlessly or simply resign to not do it at all. Once the morning comes, so does this need, the noise, the light, the clarity remind me that I exist in a shared context—I shelter in fear, away from a sort of self-constructed panopticon.

Even though fear is uncomfortable, we find comfort in it because of its vastness, its stability, and its familiarity. It’s like staying in a toxic relationship because we would rather endure that persistent discomfort than find out the one that comes with being alone. It eventually becomes a baseline; it becomes unfathomable to conceive a life without it, but it keeps you stuck. We settle for fear because it’s familiar, but behind this mirage of stability is just stagnation. To grow, we have to be willing to be seen trying—and potentially failing—even when the sun comes up.

The Healing Power of Social Friction on Noema

Essay arguing that social friction—under controlled circumstances—can be deeply constructive and transformative. Through the story of a karaoke bar, we see how bridging divides and expanding empathy by engaging with different perspectives can promote a healthy, functioning democracy.

This piece reminded me of those nights out where it feels like you’re in a fever dream, encountering almost caricature-like characters, where nothing feels quite real because you step out of the predetermination of your perception of reality. You exchange glances, toasts, and words with people that you would have never come across otherwise. Dim lights seem to blur boundaries, and everyone suddenly has their guard down; conversations flow in a way that would hardly ever feel natural in any other place.

I treat these types of nights out as field studies, dissecting every interaction to try to understand other people’s points of view. More often than not, I encounter people whose reality is entirely different than mine, and even though I can be quick to judge, I take this as an opportunity to participate in constructive discussions. Even though these interactions are fleeting—and often uncomfortable—they have allowed me to become a more empathetic person and see the nuances that lie beyond the rigidity of the things that separate us once the sun starts to rise.

Visual video essay exploring Emerson’s concept of self-reliance, highlighting the role instincts play within the tension between external validation and the innate inner voice that responds to it.

Does essence precede existence, or does existence precede essence? This is one of the age-old questions that philosophy and religion have attempted to answer across centuries and even millennia. I’m not here to attempt to answer it, but rather to nudge you to ask yourself where you believe your sense of self comes from—and I think this video is a great place to start.

I’ve said it a million times and I’ll say it again: we do not exist in a vacuum. We are exposed to endless stimuli; through the senses, we receive constant information. But what determines what makes a lasting impact and what doesn’t? It’s easy to accept whatever seems strongest, loudest, or brightest; these catch our attention, and we accept them for the sake of conformity. Our inner voices get muffled and we lose faith in ourselves. We forget that the most important voice is not always the loudest one, but the quiet one that evokes a feeling of genuine comfort and trust.

Essay exploring how we create alternate versions of ourselves and our lives in search of a sense of oneness in our inherent otherness, attempting to close the space between others and ourselves through internal projections.

A coping mechanism I’ve developed throughout the years is creating alter egos of myself—I suppose I could call these mirror-selves. When I began to realize how disturbingly alienated I felt from everyone else, I started to clone myself to try to fill the holes made by the things that I didn’t have. This multiplicity eased the perception of otherness from the people around me, but exacerbated the one I felt within myself. The price of blending in for the sake of other people’s comfort was losing my own sense of self.

Not long ago, I was talking to my best friend and told her that I feel like Hannah Montana—leading a sort of double life where I’m kind of a loser in one and a superstar in the other. This superstar alter ego is magnetic and charming; she can get anything she wants and everyone around her will make it happen. She always knows exactly what to say and to whom. She rarely ever comes out anymore because she’s not real; she’s an illusion, one that can be seen through if stared at for a bit too long.

Being her brings temporary relief but lasting emptiness, so I’ve tried to embrace other versions of myself—ones that feel more real, even if they’re not. As I’ve come to terms with my inherent otherness, especially after figuring out that I’m neurodivergent, I’ve slowly started to narrow down my mirror-selves in an attempt to figure out which one is actually me, rather than just reflections of the things I lack.

body.content: Welcome everyone to this week’s media roundup! As the new year draws closer every day, I’ve been thinking about the growth that I aspire to continue experiencing next year. Whenever I feel like I’ve grown through knowledge, relationships, experiences, or reflections, a sense of comfort fills every last corner of myself. However, the hard truth is that, to get there, you must endure the discomfort of growing pains. Finding a balance between the two isn’t always easy, but this week’s pieces certainly served as a guiding light as I get ready to embark on new journeys, so I hope they can do the same for you :)
Navigating the Mysteries on Emergence Magazine

Essay arguing that uncertainty must be navigated through mythmaking rather than punditry—creating stories to create meaningful engagement with mystery can allow us to navigate ambiguity by cultivating richness, wonder, and authenticity.

Being back home in my childhood bedroom feels like stepping into a portal that can bend the space-time fabric, allowing me to simultaneously see the many versions of myself that have existed within these four walls. I look at them and see how I have always had a rebellious tendency, this inherent impulse to question everything around me, but when I realized that this often brought me trouble so I tried my best to shut it down. Over the years, this repression turned into certainty—I now see how skepticism and stubbornness kept me stuck in loops of self-limiting beliefs. I was convinced that life existed at face value, that I was bound by the realities that everyone projected onto me.

The moment when all this pressure was finally released was in my high school philosophy class, where not only was questioning everything accepted, but it was also encouraged and even rewarded. A central theme explored in this class was Greek mythology. At first, I thought these stories were slightly ridiculous yet entertaining, but eventually, as I began analyzing and dissecting their symbolism, my world was infinitely expanded. Every myth—as fantastical as it was—gave me a framework to not only navigate the paradoxes within myself, but to pull me out of my head and place me in a context to embrace the mysteries in the world around me.

As I went down this memory lane, I came across my final essay for the subject; it was about the origins of Greek mythology and their social, political, and theological implications across time. I hadn’t read it in years, but looking back, it was a seed that bloomed into the way in which I understand the world around me. Through a lot of deconstruction and humility, I’ve learned to step out of dogmatic belief systems and embrace storytelling in the way I choose to write the narrative of my life. I’ll leave you with a quote from the closing paragraph of this very essay:
“I learned how valuable it truly is to immerse oneself in a story, since one never really knows what worlds of ideas and reflections could be discovered.”
Things To Do Instead of Doomscrolling - Feeling Human
Recommendation list created by me, where I share things to do instead of doomscrolling such as activities, books, shows, films, fun corners of the internet, and more :)
Here is the intro to this week’s installment:

If I could describe this past year in one word, it would be uncanny. With AI seeping through every crack of our lives, headlines that read like fever dreams, and the algorithmic flattening of identity—and honestly, I could go on—everything seems to be glazed by a layer of synthetic static. This feeling has led to a collective detachment and isolation, pushing us even deeper into our individualistic holes. That’s why it’s more important than ever to remind ourselves of the unique qualities that make us human. As a way to ground myself in this strange moment in time, I have made a conscious effort to embrace the connection with my own humanity and that of others, because that’s the only way we will remain as one.
Podcast exploring the concept of comfort and discomfort through the lens of certain philosophers across history, and how these can be understood in a contemporary context.

“Comfort lies at the edge of awareness”is a quote that kept buzzing in my head even long after I finished listening to this podcast—it led me to question the concept of awareness itself. Is it an oxymoron to say that we aren’t necessarily aware of what we are aware of? Maybe. My point is that perhaps, only once we accept the discomfort of the unknown, we allow for clarity of what is and isn’t held by the bounds of our awareness—of what the terrain of our comfort zone actually looks like.

This idea of understanding the self through edges has been fundamental to my growth over this past year. I remember I drew a couple of diagrams that helped me make sense of this concept a while ago. Funnily enough, the very evolution between one diagram and the other paralleled the one of expanding my comfort zone through an exploration into the unknown. The first diagram was drawn at the moment in which this idea came to my mind, and the second, once I had made room for uncomfortable concepts and experiences to develop the initial thought:

I’m not sure if these will make sense to anyone other than myself, but I wanted to illustrate how pushing our boundaries creates expansion. For me, this process wasn’t easy, but figuring out how to get to this edge has definitely been worth it.

title: Media To Consume This Week Instead of Doomscrolling: Webs of Connection
subtitle: Notes on individualism, the universe, systems thinking, perspective, mycelial networks and dreams, emergence, home, and more.
author: mapuc
raw_article:
Hello everyone and welcome to this week’s roundup! I’ve noticed that the more I learn and the deeper I dig, the more connections I discover. I was on a flight recently and it was the middle of the night, I could see how the stars in the sky mirrored the lights down on earth—this drove me into a bit of a rabbit hole to try to understand where we fit in the bigger picture. These pieces felt very expansive to me so I hope they can do the same for you :)

Subscribe now

Why Individualism Fails to Create Individuals on The Hedgehog Review

Essay arguing that independent thinking and individuality can truly only arise through a deep, trust-based process of engaging and learning from communal knowledge, authority, and tradition.

How ironic is it that in this so-called age of individualism we seem to be more homogeneous than ever? Our illusion of individuality often masks conformity; at a time when everything is simply handed to us, our egos blind us to the fact that the humble process of learning is the only way to develop self-creation. An example that comes to mind is how many “how to develop your personal style” guides quite literally present formulas for individuality, bypassing the long process of self-discovery through lived experience, and most importantly, through learning.

We are stuck in echo chambers that continuously validate this illusion, where everyone is too focused on themselves to notice that we are all recycling the same ideas over and over again. We are collections of thoughts shaped by our perception of the world—but when this perception is filtered through algorithms and closed contexts that group us into categories, we begin to mirror everyone else within them. Stuck within these feedback loops, we feed cyclical trends and consumerism—identity is then marketed as something that can be bought rather than formed.

If we don’t question these contexts and intentionally seek out unfamiliar ones by learning from and engaging with authorities across different disciplines, movements, places, histories, and traditions, we lose the ability to form individual taste. Learning alone isn’t enough, though; internalizing information to the point at which we can discern ideas that are truly meaningful to us and make connections between them is the only way we can develop a true sense of self.

Things To do This Week Instead of Doomscrolling - Gaining Perspective

Recommendation list created by me, where I share things to do instead of doomscrolling such as activities, books, shows, films, fun corners of the internet, and more.

Here is the intro to this week’s installment:

Ever since I can remember, I’ve always been a window-seat kind of person when I’m traveling. There’s something so sublime about looking out the window while you’re flying and seeing how tiny everything looks—a feeling of smallness within the vastness of the world. I love how, by zooming out, we can gain perspective and get a glimpse of how we fit into the larger picture, away from our individualistic tendencies and the tunnel vision we collectively fall into. I’m finally back home for the holidays and couldn’t sleep a wink during my entire ten-hour flight, so much of that time was spent not only looking out the window but also doing a few things that I’ll share with you here.

Podcast featuring an interview with Stephon Alexander, a theoretical physicist at Brown University who is also a professional jazz saxophonist. They discuss the parallels between the structure of music and the one of universe as a way of understanding reality through interdisciplinary frameworks.

I was never good at physics, science, or math growing up; as a matter of fact, I actually hated them. They always felt so foreign to me—whenever I attempted to understand them, I just left feeling even dumber than before. Between bad teachers and failed tests, I completely retracted from these fields the second that I could, taking refuge in literature and philosophy.

When I was little, I loved learning about outer space—always watching movies about space travel with my dad and documentaries about the universe. I even watched Interstellar in theaters when I was eleven, and it was genuinely life changing. After this spark of curiosity was dimmed by the education system for a few years, I realized what a disservice it was to myself to abandon something I used to be so fascinated by simply because I felt incapable of actually understanding it.

The issue I faced was never that I was too dumb to understand these topics; it was the way in which I was learning about them. I’m not saying that I can solve complex equations or derive physical laws from first principles—I actually don’t care about that—but through my love for and grounding in the humanities, I have been able to explore what I do care about. This podcast encapsulates exactly how powerful this interdisciplinary framework can be.

The Career Archetypes by Joel Uili
The two stages of mastery for those with many passions
When envisioning Leonardo Da Vinci we may conjure up images of him perfecting the details in John the Baptist’s index finger as it pointed towards the transcendent or selecting ultramarine paint for the blue robed figure seated next to Jesus in the last supper…
Read more
9 months ago · 5056 likes · 101 comments · Joel Uili

Guide providing a framework through examples in order to understand how individuals with many passions can navigate them to create a path of multi-disciplinary exploration and mastery.

As you may have noticed if you’ve been here a while, I am a person of many passions. I’m drawn to literature, philosophy, fashion, media literacy, internet culture, technology, ecology, cosmology, feminism, mythology—and honestly, I could go on. A big struggle for me has always been creating leaner models of learning for myself, ones that allow me to navigate breadth and then reach depth without feeling restrained. As the author shares in this piece, though, there is the multi-passionate stage and the multi-disciplinary stage—both of which are essential to this breadth-to-depth pipeline.

The way in which I’ve personally been able to move from one stage and into the other has been through systems thinking. This is a holistic way of understanding complexity that focuses on how the different elements of a whole interact, relate, and influence one another, rather than being understood in isolation. Through pattern recognition within specific contexts, it becomes possible to reveal the inner workings of a structure, and even uncover its fundamental flaws or mysteries.

Once I embraced this way of thinking, the actions required toward interdisciplinary mastery began to feel almost intuitive. The connections I make between my fields of interest are what pave the way toward depth—it’s then up to me to surrender to the journey in which these paths will take me. The wonderful thing about this is that even when I’m not an expert in something at first, having a robust foundation in other areas allows me to situate it within a wider network of ideas. These preexisting frameworks of perception and interpretation can be transferred not only to flatten the learning curve, but also to help me discern what is relevant to me and what is not. As long as I remain present and immersed in these natural streams of exploration, creation inevitably follows.

Dreams and Mycelium: Mapping the Endless Network of Existence

Essay exploring how mycelial science, Freud’s dream theory, and Indigenous knowledge intertwine, arguing that life is an interconnected web where dreams, the natural world, and our ancestors pass down generational memories that shape reality.

I’ve been learning a lot about fractals lately, these are infinitely complex geometric shapes which exhibit self-similarity where repeating patterns exist in different scales. An example of this are the recurring geometries that repeat across natural and and bodily systems—how veins resemble river systems, how lungs resemble tree branches, how a DNA helix resembles spiral galaxies, and how neurons resemble mycelial networks. Approaching mycelial networks through an analogical framework reveals patterns of interdependence that extend far beyond the natural world, extending into the world of knowledge, memories, and even consciousness.

I don’t believe these are mere coincidences, we are all part of one big system, it’s just a matter of zooming in and out of these fractals to realize how everything is connected. I found it so interesting to see this through the perspective of dreams. According to Freud:

“The dream-thoughts to which we are led by interpretation, cannot, from the nature of things, have any definite endings; they are bound to branch out in every direction into the intricate network of our world of thought. It is at some point where this meshwork is particularly close that the dream-wish grows up, like a mushroom out of its mycelium.”

This metaphor really stood out to me because of the uncertainty surrounding these networks and the mystery of their origins within the subconscious—the ideas that arise from dreams may therefore allude to a form of collective consciousness. The dream functions as a singular point of origin from which ideas unfold, much like a spore gives rise to a mycelial network, a seed to a forest, or a cosmic singularity to the universe.

Video essay explaining how the complexity of things in our universe is actually built from very simple elements, following basic rules. Through computer simulations and examples from nature, we explore the process of emergence as a phenomenon and practice essential for creation.

The process of emergence is a fundamental one in every single aspect of origination, be that of an idea, a life, an element, or even computational systems. If we think of this concept through the metaphor of the garden, the seeds are the building blocks, but they won’t sprout without soil or water, which we can understand as the laws of nature. Creating an emergent system is less like being an architect and more like being a gardener, allowing organized complexity to flower on its own, bound by certain universal rules.

The concept of this complex organization drove me to ask how it might relate to a state of complex disorganization—also understood as entropy. What I found is that while emergence creates order, it will eventually decay or collapse; however, this very dispersal is what allows new emergent patterns to arise. Coming back to the metaphor of the flower, its life cycle from sprout to decay—from emergence to entropy—is what creates the fertile ground for more life to grow. However, we need to be careful about which plants we decide to water, because not only will they shape current and future systems, but they may also destabilize or destroy the ones already in place.

toni's curiosity cabinet
on moving house and the anthropology of home
If you’ve been enjoying the work here, I’d love it if you considered becoming a paid subscriber. Your support helps me keep researching, writing, and sharing these ideas…
Read more
10 months ago · 167 likes · 16 comments · toni ꩜

Essay exploring the relational nature of the nomadic experience and the perception of home through research on attachment, physical spaces, domesticity, transition, and more.

Since I was very young, I knew I wanted to leave the place I called home. I was never overly attached to it, given that I never felt a true sense of belonging. I felt misunderstood by most of the people around me. I tried my best to fit into the strange power dynamics and social hierarchies, but it always felt unnatural to me. Unfortunately, many aspects of Colombian life were not compatible with my way of being, my understanding of the world, or what I wanted out of life. It’s a bizarre feeling to feel foreign in the place where you’re supposed to belong.

One of my favorite songs when I was much younger is called Home by Gabrielle Aplin. I was around ten when I first found it, and given that I barely spoke English, I only had a vague idea of what it was about, yet there was something unexplainable that connected me to it. As the years went by and I eventually moved abroad, the song began to take form. This made me realize that the reason it sparked something within me all those years ago was because it was, in a way, a self-fulfilling prophecy. I still don’t know exactly what home means to me, but I know it’s not a place.

I’m not saying that I can solve complex equations or derive physical laws from first principles—I actually don’t care about that—but through my love for and grounding in the humanities, I have been able to explore what I do care about. This podcast encapsulates exactly how powerful this interdisciplinary framework can be.

If you value the time and care that goes into my work and want to join a community of learners, consider becoming a paid subscriber <3
Guide providing a framework through examples in order to understand how individuals with many passions can navigate them to create a path of multi-disciplinary exploration and mastery.

As you may have noticed if you’ve been here a while, I am a person of many passions. I’m drawn to literature, philosophy, fashion, media literacy, internet culture, technology, ecology, cosmology, feminism, mythology—and honestly, I could go on. A big struggle for me has always been creating leaner models of learning for myself, ones that allow me to navigate breadth and then reach depth without feeling restrained. As the author shares in this piece, though, there is the multi-passionate stage and the multi-disciplinary stage—both of which are essential to this breadth-to-depth pipeline.

The way in which I’ve personally been able to move from one stage and into the other has been through systems thinking. This is a holistic way of understanding complexity that focuses on how the different elements of a whole interact, relate, and influence one another, rather than being understood in isolation. Through pattern recognition within specific contexts, it becomes possible to reveal the inner workings of a structure, and even uncover its fundamental flaws or mysteries.

Once I embraced this way of thinking, the actions required toward interdisciplinary mastery began to feel almost intuitive. The connections I make between my fields of interest are what pave the way toward depth—it’s then up to me to surrender to the journey in which these paths will take me. The wonderful thing about this is that even when I’m not an expert in something at first, having a robust foundation in other areas allows me to situate it within a wider network of ideas. These preexisting frameworks of perception and interpretation can be transferred not only to flatten the learning curve, but also to help me discern what is relevant to me and what is not. As long as I remain present and immersed in these natural streams of exploration, creation inevitably follows.
Dreams and Mycelium: Mapping the Endless Network of Existence

Essay exploring how mycelial science, Freud’s dream theory, and Indigenous knowledge intertwine, arguing that life is an interconnected web where dreams, the natural world, and our ancestors pass down generational memories that shape reality.

I’ve been learning a lot about fractals lately, these are infinitely complex geometric shapes which exhibit self-similarity where repeating patterns exist in different scales. An example of this are the recurring geometries that repeat across natural and and bodily systems—how veins resemble river systems, how lungs resemble tree branches, how a DNA helix resembles spiral galaxies, and how neurons resemble mycelial networks. Approaching mycelial networks through an analogical framework reveals patterns of interdependence that extend far beyond the natural world, extending into the world of knowledge, memories, and even consciousness.

I don’t believe these are mere coincidences, we are all part of one big system, it’s just a matter of zooming in and out of these fractals to realize how everything is connected. I found it so interesting to see this through the perspective of dreams. According to Freud:

“The dream-thoughts to which we are led by interpretation, cannot, from the nature of things, have any definite endings; they are bound to branch out in every direction into the intricate network of our world of thought. It is at some point where this meshwork is particularly close that the dream-wish grows up, like a mushroom out of its mycelium.”

This metaphor really stood out to me because of the uncertainty surrounding these networks and the mystery of their origins within the subconscious—the ideas that arise from dreams may therefore allude to a form of collective consciousness. The dream functions as a singular point of origin from which ideas unfold, much like a spore gives rise to a mycelial network, a seed to a forest, or a cosmic singularity to the universe.

Video essay explaining how the complexity of things in our universe is actually built from very simple elements, following basic rules. Through computer simulations and examples from nature, we explore the process of emergence as a phenomenon and practice essential for creation.

The process of emergence is a fundamental one in every single aspect of origination, be that of an idea, a life, an element, or even computational systems. If we think of this concept through the metaphor of the garden, the seeds are the building blocks, but they won’t sprout without soil or water, which we can understand as the laws of nature. Creating an emergent system is less like being an architect and more like being a gardener, allowing organized complexity to flower on its own, bound by certain universal rules.

The concept of this complex organization drove me to ask how it might relate to a state of complex disorganization—also understood as entropy. What I found is that while emergence creates order, it will eventually decay or collapse; however, this very dispersal is what allows new emergent patterns to arise. Coming back to the metaphor of the flower, its life cycle from sprout to decay—from emergence to entropy—is what creates the fertile ground for more life to grow. However, we need to be careful about which plants we decide to water, because not only will they shape current and future systems, but they may also destabilize or destroy the ones already in place.
Essay exploring the relational nature of the nomadic experience and the perception of home through research on attachment, physical spaces, domesticity, transition, and more.

Since I was very young, I knew I wanted to leave the place I called home. I was never overly attached to it, given that I never felt a true sense of belonging. I felt misunderstood by most of the people around me. I tried my best to fit into the strange power dynamics and social hierarchies, but it always felt unnatural to me. Unfortunately, many aspects of Colombian life were not compatible with my way of being, my understanding of the world, or what I wanted out of life. It’s a bizarre feeling to feel foreign in the place where you’re supposed to belong.

One of my favorite songs when I was much younger is calledHomeby Gabrielle Aplin. I was around ten when I first found it, and given that I barely spoke English, I only had a vague idea of what it was about, yet there was something unexplainable that connected me to it. As the years went by and I eventually moved abroad, the song began to take form. This made me realize that the reason it sparked something within me all those years ago was because it was, in a way, a self-fulfilling prophecy. I still don’t know exactly what home means to me, but I know it’s not a place.

title: Books to Read This Month Instead of Doomscrolling
subtitle: What I’ve been reading before the year ends, brought to you by Book of the Month
author: mapuc
raw_article:
In this last stretch of the year, I have found much refuge in books. The temperature has dropped, responsibilities are piling up, the holidays are coming soon, and a linger of existential dread can be felt in the air. There is so much joy and excitement as we begin to close the chapter of this past year, but also a lot of uncertainty as we get ready to face the change that is coming.

Experiencing the ambivalence of life in such a heightened way inevitably prevents us from feeling things clearly, and to me, books are the anchor that allows me to make sense of those feelings. Even though I am usually a very structured reader, chaotic moments often need to be matched—this means that I’ve been reading multiple books at the same time and experimenting with different formats, which has honestly been so refreshing.

You’ll notice that this month’s selections span across a wide array of genres, topics, and even time periods. I find that expanding in this way helps me gain some perspective as I attempt to see the bigger picture. I don’t read to escape; I read to find myself, so when I speak of the bigger picture, I’m always referring to the one in which I manage to see my reflection. If you’re feeling anything like me during this time, I hope you find a few reads that can make things a little easier for you too.

Today’s post is sponsored by Book of The Month. As always, I only recommend things that I genuinely think you will love :)

Two of the books in this month’s roundup are from Book of the Month, a monthly subscription service that puts together a curated list of new fiction releases to choose from in either hardback or audio format. Picking books can be a really overwhelming task so having this option is a great way to cut through decision fatigue! If nothing in the monthly lineup piques your interest, you can skip the month, and if you end up not liking the one you picked, their Reader’s Guarantee lets you replace it for free in your next box.

If you are looking for a gift for a reader in your life, BOTM is a lovely choice. They offer 3, 6, and 12-month plans, and you can send them as a physical card, a print-at-home option, or by email for instant delivery which is perfect for those last-minute gifts—holiday gifts also come with a free hat :)

You can use my link to get your first book for $5 plus a free gift with code HOLLY <3

Nausea by Jean Paul Sartre

This philosophical novel follows Antoine Roquentin, a historian living in a provincial French town, grappling with a disorienting sense of alienation and existential dread. As he goes about his daily life, he notices how the world around him is beginning to dissolve into a pool of meaninglessness—objects, people, places, and routines are now obsolete. We go on an existential journey in which we must face the idea that life may be inherently void of meaning, and perhaps, what we can do about it.

I first read this book when I was around seventeen, and it felt like one of the biggest slaps in the face I had experienced up to that point. It awoke something in me that, quite honestly, I think is the reason I am sitting here today, writing for all of you beautiful people. Coming back to it years later has been an emotional rollercoaster, but it felt right as I’m figuring out what I want to do with my life. Back then, my teenage self was going through a very difficult time; not only was she struggling deeply with her mental health, but she was also trying her best to make it through the pandemic in one piece—all while trying to graduate high school. Even though this book is quite literally about an existential crisis, it made me feel seen because it put so many of my discombobulated and confusing feelings into words. The thing about existentialism is that once we realize that, if life has no inherent meaning, we are able to create our own.

Best Offer Wins by Marisa Kashino

This BOTM pick is a contemporary fiction novel about the insanely competitive housing market in Washington, D.C., where 37-year-old publicist Margo Miyake is on the quest to secure her perfect house. After 18 months and 11 failed bids, she gets a tip about a house that hasn’t hit the open market yet, and she becomes willing to go to extreme lengths to get it before it’s publicly listed. Feeling as though this is the only way to get her life on the right track, we get to explore themes of class, ambition, and desire as we see her push past the line between persistence and obsession.

I recently started this book, and it felt like a breath of fresh air against the heavier psychological and philosophical classics I’ve been reading as of late—don’t get me wrong, I have absolutely adored them, but they do tend to pull me out of reality and into beautifully strange existential spirals. This story has brought me right back into the real world, as it not only deals with the absurdity of navigating adulthood in this day and age but has also provided some much-needed laughs. I’m feeling like the training wheels of adulthood are coming off soon; I’m about to start apartment hunting to figure out where I’ll live after graduation, so this book feels like the perfect prelude to this new era of my life.

Fictions by Jorge Luis Borges

A revolutionary collection of short stories that bend reality, identity, and narrative as we find ourselves in this fantastical universe. Labyrinths, fictional scholars, impossible books, and alternate worlds drive us to question the very fabric of reality. These are not just stories—they’re thought experiments that allow us to explore how memory, truth, choice, language, and knowledge construct meaning in stories and in life.

You know when you’re reading a book and you start to come to the realization that it will fundamentally change your life? That’s exactly how I’ve felt making my way through these stories—so much so that I haven’t been able to bring myself to finish it yet, just to savor it a little longer. It’s hard to describe the essence of Borges’s universe; it is a wonderfully surreal space in which logic wavers and reality bends in every possible direction. The most intriguing and captivating thing about these stories is that they often read like nonfiction, which weirdly sets them in a parallel universe to ours. This perspective has led me to question so many aspects of my reality—and the fact that creating it is often up to us.

Before I Forget by Tory Henwood Hoen

This BOTM pick is a late coming-of-age novel that tells the story of 26-year-old Cricket Campbell as she goes through a quarter-life crisis and returns home to care for her father after his Alzheimer’s diagnosis—this decision forces her to confront past trauma, family estrangement, and grief. As her father’s memory unravels, he becomes capable of predicting the future, all while Cricket attempts to piece her own memory back together.

As I read the first few pages of this book, I realized I had to save it for when I head back home for the holidays—the premise aligns a little too well with the emotional whirlwinds that usually happen during this time of year, along with some difficult situations in my family. December has always made me reminisce about the past, from reflecting on the craziness of the year to grieving the losses of what I’ve been forced to leave behind. As I’ve built a life away from home, going back feels like entering a haunted house; I see the ghosts of my past everywhere I look, especially the ones I’ve tried my best to avoid. I’m really looking forward to reading this when I’m home, hoping that Cricket’s experience makes me feel a little less lonely as I go through my own.

The Nun by Denis Diderot

This 18th-century novel is presented as a series of letters written by Suzanne Simonin, a young woman who is forced into a convent due to her family’s reputation. These letters begin as an attempt to resist her vows but then turn into a detailed account of the torture she endures because of the institutional control within the convents she is forced to live in. Suzanne exposes the cruelty, manipulation, and problematic power dynamics within religious life, all while she attempts to assert her own will when everything around her tries to break it.

This time of year inevitably makes me confront my relationship with religion, given that I spend the holidays with my extremely Catholic family. I was raised to be very religious, not just because of my family but because of my all-girls Catholic school—regardless of everyone’s efforts, it unfortunately didn’t work. It’s not that I hold anything against religion; so much of it has been fundamental to my identity. However, I refuse to ignore the systemic issues that make the Church one of the most powerful institutions on Earth. Leaving my personal beliefs aside, this book is such a subversive and all-consuming exploration of so many things that organized religions fail to address. Having this perspective from within gave me clarity on a lot of the things I had been exploring, especially as I attempt to understand spirituality separate from religious institutions.

title: Media To Consume This Week Instead of Doomscrolling: Reclaiming Our Humanity
subtitle: Notes on art, uncertainty, dreaming, the mind-body problem, circularity, surrealism, and more.
author: mapuc
raw_article:
Welcome everyone to this week’s media roundup! Lately, I have been thinking about what it means to be human, which especially relevant when so much of our reality seems to try to dehumanize us. A lot of much needed existential reflections were had this week but I hope they help you feel a little more human.

Video essay arguing that in this current landscape, where the humanities have been devalued, and culture seems to be collapsing, art is the key to a cultural rebirth that can lead toward a future worth fighting for.

Something I have struggled with my entire life is the feeling of disconnection. This destabilizing state of entropy keeps me away from the world around me, the people in it, and even from myself. This feeling isn’t permanent, though. Sometimes I feel a sense of wholeness, a momentary overlap between the abstraction of the soul and the physicality of the present; a sense of embodiment. These fleeting moments often come when I am able to form an emotional link between myself and something I consider art, be that a painting, a book, a building, an experience, or even a concept. This link is ultimately forged by the passion that fuels the human experience and our inherent need for creation.

Especially after the Industrial Revolution, we began to devalue the humanities as technological and scientific breakthroughs took center stage, making STEM-related fields the only ones worth pursuing under capitalism. Something we seem to ignore, though, is how none of these revolutionary breakthroughs would have happened without the humanities. One great example of this is cosmology—the study of the origin and development of the universe—because it transitioned from a purely philosophical pursuit to a modern scientific field through the integration of empirical evidence and mathematical frameworks such as Einstein’s theory of relativity.

The term humanities is derived from the Latin “studia humanitatis,” which was used during the Renaissance to describe the studies of human nature. We are being disconnected from our nature as a way to cope in an unnaturally built society where money is the universal god that we are taught to worship. As easy as it is to surrender to the magnitude of evil we are currently experiencing, art is the essential key to connecting with something larger than ourselves and creating a purposeful reality.

Things To Do Instead of Doomscrolling - Seeking Comfort

Recommendation list created by me, where I share things to do instead of doomscrolling such as activities, books, shows, films, fun corners of the internet, and more.

Here is the intro to this week’s installment:

For some reason, it feels as if all of the unfinished business that was started this year is desperately trying to come to a close. I’m not very fond of the last couple of months before the new year because they inevitably force me to confront change—whether I like it or not. Even though it feels as though chaos is taking over and the upcoming year is sprinting toward me, I know that there’s actually a lot I can do to ground myself and slow down.

In pursuit of the hole on Aeon

Essay exploring how absences created by holes—both physical and conceptual—create a map to understand the past and present by generating action toward discovering what is missing.

There is this idea that has been brewing in my head over the past year. I don’t think I can put my finger on it just quite yet, but essentially, I’m exploring the duality of being. If we understand the concept of being from a linear perspective—one where there is a beginning—this means that we must grapple with the paradoxical idea of nothingness. Something coming into a state of being is then inextricably linked to its state of non-being. This void of nothingness is fundamentally shaped by the absence of something.

This may sound like a slightly abstract idea, but it’s actually one of the pillars through which we are able to create. This abstraction of nothingness becomes tangible once we create frameworks for understanding it; the essay presents some of these seen through art, books, photography, literature, and history. The void that is present across these fields is one that we can scale across all facets of perception. On one hand, we can zoom in and out from the objective to the subjective—think of physical holes in books to conceptual holes in their content. On the other hand, we can zoom in and out from the micro to the macro—think of the holes in our individual knowledge of the universe to the black holes that actually exist within it.

Our lack of awareness of these voids doesn’t mean that they don’t exist, but only once we acknowledge them can we begin to create ways of actually seeing what lies within them.

Museguided
Dreaming While the World Burns
Read more
5 months ago · 232 likes · 113 comments · Tamara

Essay arguing that even though dreaming feels selfish or foolish, given the stark state of the world, it is the essential human quality that allows us not to surrender to despair and move toward a future built by us rather than imposed onto us.

Operating on pure logic has never made sense to me. I find it silly to accept a version of reality perceived through the microscope of certainty. How unfulfilling is it to go about life without attempting to decipher the uncertain, not only the one the world around us, but especially the one of our existence within it? Dreaming of seemingly impossible realities fueled by our desire for something that we lack is the moment of conception in which possibilities are born.

There is this disheartening unbalance between logos and pathos, which has led us to strip ourselves of our humanity, turning us into ego-driven individuals as a way to cope in a materialistic and individualistic society. So much of our humanity lies in our need to dream, but we are being shamed into its repression due to the disparities of the current dystopian landscape. I see dreaming as a privilege because even though everyone can dream, many can’t afford to do so because survival takes this away from them. This is exactly why we must dare to dream, not just to build a better future for ourselves, but for the ones who can’t.

The Mind’s Body Problem on NYRB

Essay outlining how different thinkers and perspectives from antiquity into the present day conceive the mind-body relationship, and how the human desire for immortality persists on a human exceptionalism perspective that insists that the body can be edited and even separated from the mind.

I went to a Louise Bourgeois exhibition once; she is one of my favorite artists, so I took myself to the museum to finally experience her art in the flesh. While observing some of her red gouache paintings in which she explores motherhood and the inherent violence of the female body, I came to an unsettling realization. The words “my body is the parasite of my mind” echoed in my head as I made my way through the corridors. I saw myself in fragments, shattered pieces of something that was once whole—I was no longer able to perceive the fundamental link between the mind and the body.

I think this sentiment is a symptom of the disconnection that I spoke about earlier—the disconnection between our nature and the one around us. The anthropocentric view of these natures being different doesn’t actually make sense because we are one and the same, regardless of how much we try to create this separation. In this essay, we see how our superiority complex leads to concerns about eugenic and transhumanist pipelines, ones that seek to take away the very essence of life: death.

Podcast featuring an interview with renowned author and botanist Robin Wall Kimmerer, where they discuss the possibilities of a gift economy in which we exist in symbiosis with the Earth by taking only what we need, sharing abundance, and offering gratitude.

The Earth operates under a system of circularity, one in which regeneration is the fuel for the movement in the cycle of life. Why is it then that we decided that this model wasn’t good enough for us? There are many answers to this, but essentially, we have decided that short-term rewards such as convenience and profit are worth more than the longevity and well-being of humanity—all at the expense of our environment. Our selfishness has turned us into a short-sighted society, incapable of seeing beyond ourselves.

This idea of a gift economy is a beautiful way in which, through biomimicry, we return to this circular model of the Earth. Through reverence and gratitude, we are able to connect with the Earth, our communities, and our human nature. It’s not easy to picture how this could actually look, especially under the pre-existing systems, which go directly against this model, but it’s not as far-fetched as it seems to be. In this piece, we get to explore different depictions of gender within the genre, which is truly just the start. body.content: Welcome everyone to this week’s media roundup! Lately, I have been thinking about what it means to be human, which especially relevant when so much of our reality seems to try to dehumanize us. A lot of much needed existential reflections were had this week but I hope they help you feel a little more human.

Video essay arguing that in this current landscape, where the humanities have been devalued, and culture seems to be collapsing, art is the key to a cultural rebirth that can lead toward a future worth fighting for.

Something I have struggled with my entire life is the feeling of disconnection. This destabilizing state of entropy keeps me away from the world around me, the people in it, and even from myself. This feeling isn’t permanent, though. Sometimes I feel a sense of wholeness, a momentary overlap between the abstraction of the soul and the physicality of the present; a sense of embodiment. These fleeting moments often come when I am able to form an emotional link between myself and something I consider art, be that a painting, a book, a building, an experience, or even a concept. This link is ultimately forged by the passion that fuels the human experience and our inherent need for creation.

Especially after the Industrial Revolution, we began to devalue the humanities as technological and scientific breakthroughs took center stage, making STEM-related fields the only ones worth pursuing under capitalism. Something we seem to ignore, though, is how none of these revolutionary breakthroughs would have happened without the humanities. One great example of this is cosmology—the study of the origin and development of the universe—because it transitioned from a purely philosophical pursuit to a modern scientific field through the integration of empirical evidence and mathematical frameworks such as Einstein’s theory of relativity.

The termhumanitiesis derived from the Latin “studia humanitatis,” which was used during the Renaissance to describe the studies of human nature. We are being disconnected from our nature as a way to cope in an unnaturally built society where money is the universal god that we are taught to worship. As easy as it is to surrender to the magnitude of evil we are currently experiencing, art is the essential key to connecting with something larger than ourselves and creating a purposeful reality.
Things To Do Instead of Doomscrolling - Seeking Comfort
Recommendation list created by me, where I share things to do instead of doomscrolling such as activities, books, shows, films, fun corners of the internet, and more.
Here is the intro to this week’s installment:

For some reason, it feels as if all of the unfinished business that was started this year is desperately trying to come to a close. I’m not very fond of the last couple of months before the new year because they inevitably force me to confront change—whether I like it or not. Even though it feels as though chaos is taking over and the upcoming year is sprinting toward me, I know that there’s actually a lot I can do to ground myself and slow down.
In pursuit of the hole on Aeon
Essay exploring how absences created by holes—both physical and conceptual—create a map to understand the past and present by generating action toward discovering what is missing.

There is this idea that has been brewing in my head over the past year. I don’t think I can put my finger on it just quite yet, but essentially, I’m exploring the duality of being. If we understand the concept of being from a linear perspective—one where there is a beginning—this means that we must grapple with the paradoxical idea of nothingness. Something coming into a state of being is then inextricably linked to its state of non-being. This void of nothingness is fundamentally shaped by the absence of something.

This may sound like a slightly abstract idea, but it’s actually one of the pillars through which we are able to create. This abstraction of nothingness becomes tangible once we create frameworks for understanding it; the essay presents some of these seen through art, books, photography, literature, and history. The void that is present across these fields is one that we can scale across all facets of perception. On one hand, we can zoom in and out from the objective to the subjective—think of physical holes in books to conceptual holes in their content. On the other hand, we can zoom in and out from the micro to the macro—think of the holes in our individual knowledge of the universe to the black holes that actually exist within it.
Our lack of awareness of these voids doesn’t mean that they don’t exist, but only once we acknowledge them can we begin to create ways of actually seeing what lies within them.

Essay arguing that even though dreaming feels selfish or foolish, given the stark state of the world, it is the essential human quality that allows us not to surrender to despair and move toward a future built by us rather than imposed onto us.

Operating on pure logic has never made sense to me. I find it silly to accept a version of reality perceived through the microscope of certainty. How unfulfilling is it to go about life without attempting to decipher the uncertain, not only the one the world around us, but especially the one of our existence within it? Dreaming of seemingly impossible realities fueled by our desire for something that we lack is the moment of conception in which possibilities are born.

There is this disheartening unbalance betweenlogosandpathos,whichhas led us to strip ourselves of our humanity, turning us into ego-driven individuals as a way to cope in a materialistic and individualistic society. So much of our humanity lies in our need to dream, but we are being shamed into its repression due to the disparities of the current dystopian landscape. I see dreaming as a privilege because even though everyone can dream, many can’t afford to do so because survival takes this away from them. This is exactly why we must dare to dream, not just to build a better future for ourselves, but for the ones who can’t.
The Mind’s Body Problem on NYRB

Essay outlining how different thinkers and perspectives from antiquity into the present day conceive the mind-body relationship, and how the human desire for immortality persists on a human exceptionalism perspective that insists that the body can be edited and even separated from the mind.

I went to a Louise Bourgeois exhibition once; she is one of my favorite artists, so I took myself to the museum to finally experience her art in the flesh. While observing some of her red gouache paintings in which she explores motherhood and the inherent violence of the female body, I came to an unsettling realization. The words“my body is the parasite of my mind”echoed in my head as I made my way through the corridors. I saw myself in fragments, shattered pieces of something that was once whole—I was no longer able to perceive the fundamental link between the mind and the body.

I think this sentiment is a symptom of the disconnection that I spoke about earlier—the disconnection between our nature and the one around us. The anthropocentric view of these natures being different doesn’t actually make sense because weareone and the same, regardless of how much we try to create this separation. In this essay, we see how our superiority complex leads to concerns about eugenic and transhumanist pipelines, ones that seek to take away the very essence of life: death.

Podcast featuring an interview with renowned author and botanist Robin Wall Kimmerer, where they discuss the possibilities of a gift economy in which we exist in symbiosis with the Earth by taking only what we need, sharing abundance, and offering gratitude.

The Earth operates under a system of circularity, one in which regeneration is the fuel for the movement in the cycle of life. Why is it then that we decided that this model wasn’t good enough for us? There are many answers to this, but essentially, we have decided that short-term rewards such as convenience and profit are worth more than the longevity and well-being of humanity—all at the expense of our environment. Our selfishness has turned us into a short-sighted society, incapable of seeing beyond ourselves.

This idea of a gift economy is a beautiful way in which, through biomimicry, we return to this circular model of the Earth. Through reverence and gratitude, we are able to connect with the Earth, our communities, and our human nature. It’s not easy to picture how this could actually look, especially under the pre-existing systems, which go directly against this model, but it’s not as far-fetched as it seems to be. This is not something that can be scaled, but if we create strong connections with local communities, it is possible to create circular systems of support.

These two worlds are bridged by their common quality of revelation, allowing us to see the intricacies of society and the human psyche alike.
