title: The Politics of Feeling
subtitle: Notes on emotional media literacy, the architecture of disinformation, and mediated reality.
author: mapuc
raw_article:
Everything is political. I remember this realization first clicked for me in my high school philosophy class, while learning about the ancient Greek term polis—which essentially translates to city-state. As citizens of the world, our very existence and every action we take is directly related to the state, the environment, society, and our communities, and in turn, to ourselves, because we do not exist in a vacuum. We are complex and biased beings, constantly pulled between logic, emotion, and morality—so isn’t it an oxymoron when someone calls themselves apolitical?

This illusion of neutrality or absence is void of meaning—especially when it comes to the media we constantly consume, and the way it directly shapes our views, emotional responses, and identities. This is why I am such a strong advocate for media literacy, but an aspect we don’t often speak about is emotional media literacy. Media literacy goes beyond truth versus disinformation; it is also about understanding how media makes us feel, and how we respond to both information and emotion. Especially in our current mediated reality, emotions are often pre-framed for us, silently shaping our perception without us even realizing it. Feeling is political because, regardless of how intimate and personal our emotional responses are, they ultimately shape collective behavior that makes up the fabric of society.

AI is a very conflicting subject for me. Its lack of regulation and exponential growth are, frankly, frightening. I worry about us entangling it with the very things that make us human: art, connection, creativity, our environment—and life itself. Recently, I was browsing Aeon—if you’ve been here for a while, you know it’s my favorite publication—and came across an article about AI companies having developed digital avatars to simulate deceased loved ones. Safe to say, I had a very intense emotional response. Whenever I catch myself reacting this way to a piece of media, I know my ego is pushing me toward quick judgment, ultimately distorting my clarity. That’s when I know I need to step back, evaluate, and start asking questions.

How do we answer them?

Asking the questions is easy—the difficult part is learning how to answer them. During my journey of working on my media literacy, I realized that I can’t always do this on my own. This is when I came across Ground News. I’ve been a fan of Ground News for a while now, and having the opportunity to partner with them in advocating for something so important to me feels genuinely special.

Ground News has been an incredibly useful platform because they process thousands of articles daily, cutting through polarizing algorithms by making media bias visible and highlighting information blind spots all in one place. This tool—which you can access through both their website and app—has made the process of answering these questions significantly easier, as it allows you to actively exercise your critical thinking skills instead of passively consuming headlines.

Subscribe to Ground News

Comparing and contrasting

Coming back to the news about AI companies developing digital avatars to simulate deceased loved ones, I went on Ground News and was glad to see that the initial Aeon piece I read was included in their coverage. From there, I wanted to understand not just where it stood on the bias scale, but how other outlets, positioned differently across that spectrum, were framing the same story. In total, this story was covered by 26 sources worldwide.

Here’s what I noticed: while left-leaning outlets tended to emphasize the potential psychological risks and ethical implications, right-leaning outlets—which barely covered the story—framed it more as an issue of personal choice, often reduced to a “comfort or illusion” argument, downplaying specific harms. More centrist outlets, on the other hand, offered a more balanced framing and frequently referenced academic research.

I’m not here to tell you which sources you should listen to. Instead, I want you to take control over how you understand a narrative—and so does Ground News. Reality is increasingly framed through emotional narratives shaped by algorithmic systems, so we need tools that help us see beyond them.

Ground News is not backed by billionaires, corporations, or governments; they are entirely subscriber-supported, just like I am. By subscribing, you not only contribute to a more transparent media landscape, but you also support my work in continuing to grow this community. If you’re interested, you can subscribe through the link at groundnews.com/mapu or click below to get 40% off the unlimited Vantage plan, which comes out to $5 a month—the same one that I use :)

Subscribe to Ground News

By questioning the world around us, we can begin to shape a reality under our terms, one that is more transparent, connected, ethical, and empowering—and I hope Ground News becomes as useful to you as it has been for me <3
